observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE request the Sangguniang Bayan to pass a resolution authorizing the transfer of School Buildings, Barangay Health Centers to the various recipient barangays, and then prepare Deeds of Donation for the actual transfer and acceptance by the latter to substantiate the transfer of ownership of the said properties.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the BAC through the BAC Secretariat to: a. immediately submit lacking documentary requirements of the nine infrastructure projects and 12 procurements of goods identified in Annexes A and B; and procurement documents of the 12 perfected contracts of which nine had already been paid as enumerated in Annex C; b. prioritize the organization and submission of procurement documents; and c. devise mechanisms or strategies to submit the contracts and their supporting documents within five days upon completion of the contract for timely auditorial contract review by the AT and to communicate any audit results and prompt information to management of any deficiencies noticed, if any. Further, we recommended that the LCE direct the MA to stop certifying the completeness of supporting documents and subsequently process disbursement vouchers with incomplete or no supporting documents attached thereto.

observations.recommendation: Considering the limited manpower of the LGU, especially in the Accounting, Treasury, and Assessor’s Office, we have recommended the consideration and adoption of electronic or computerized systems for accounting, assessment, and collection processes. This would lead to timely and cost-efficient transaction processing and facilitate the preparation of reports, enabling management to comply and submit on time. Given the benefits it would provide to management and employees, we highly encourage the implementation of this recommendation.
observations.managementAction: The management, as part of the LGU commitment to comply with the digitization and digitalization of LGU processes, appropriated in the previous years a reasonable amount of Capital Outlay for Treasurer’s Office and Assessors Office for the planned procurement of additional IT equipment that will complement, suit and work well with system installed by the province. Recently, the province installed the PARTS to our Municipal Assessor’s Office in order to digitize our real property assessment process to be connected in the system used by our Treasurer’s Office. At present, the LGU officers and employees, both in Assessor and Treasurers Office, are familiarizing the system and will continue to undergo capability trainings to effectively used the PARTS.
observations.reason:
The PARTS was installed in the Municipal Assessor’s Office to digitize the real property assessment process and connect it to the system used by the Treasurer’s Office. However, no electronic or computerized systems have been installed yet for the Accounting and Treasury Offices.

Revised in Part II of this 2024 AAR.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer to adopt the ATM or digitized payroll system in the disbursement of payroll for salaries, wages and other emoluments of elected officials, regular and casual employees of the LGU. The adoption of a digitized payroll system is highly encouraged because it strengthens internal control on cash with improved delivery service to employees at reduced costs.
observations.managementAction:
The management communicated with the Land Bank-Carmen Rosales, as the agency’s authorized government servicing bank, the need of the LGU for ATM payroll system. 
 
As of this writing, the LGU and its employees were scheduled to open their Payroll account in the Land Bank-Carmen Rosales.
observations.reason:
As per validation, we have observed that as of this date, the ATM or digitized payroll system was not yet adopted by the LGU in the disbursement of payroll for salaries, wages and other emoluments of elected officials, regular and casual employees of the LGU.

Revised in Part II of this 2024 AAR.

observations.recommendation:

Submit copies of perfected contracts, approved POs, and supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within the prescribed period of five (5) working days pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 for a timely review and evaluation thereof. Administrative disciplinary action, as provided in Section 4 of the same circular against the persons responsible, be imposed upon unjustified failure to comply.
observations.managementAction: The management will assign personnel who will assist the concerned/ accountable officer in performing the needed task. observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management to

b)  Assess the collectability and exert efforts in the collection of the outstanding accounts and set up the appropriate Allowance for Impairment for possible losses in accordance with Paragraphs 67 and 72 of IPSAS 29; and

c)  If these receivables proved to be uncollectible and worthless, the Agency should derecognize the asset account and the corresponding allowance for impairment from the books of account through write-off of the dormant accounts according to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 - Guidelines and Procedures on the Write-off of Dormant Receivable Accounts, Unliquidated Cash Advances, Fund Transfers of NGAs, LGUs, and GOCC.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management require:
a) The Municipal Engineer to provide the Accountant and the General Services Officer with the complete description and segregation of road components for those with data on construction cost. For those not posted in the registry but without identified cost and those included in the RPCRNS but not in the registry, the fair value must be used in determining the cost of road components and thereafter shall provide the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects;

b) The Municipal Accountant to depreciate the carrying amount of existing roads and its components to reflect the asset in its used condition and accordingly adjust the amount recorded in the Road Network account supported by the RPCRNS, Registry Infrastructure for Roads and working paper on the determination of the depreciated replacement cost per registry. Subsequently, at the end of each year, a lapsing schedule for the computation of depreciation for each depreciable road component must be prepared as basis for recording depreciation expenses and the required Local Road Network Ledger Card for each road network shall likewise be maintained; and

c) The GSO at the end or every accounting period to render a Report on Local Road Network using the pro-forma schedule in Annex A of same Circular and maintain local road inventory, road map and complete road network property card for all roads and its components.
observations.managementAction: The Management did not comply with the audit recommendations.
observations.reason: This observation was reiterated in Observation No. 7, page number 43.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor together with the Inventory Committee and the other concerned offices to complete the disposition and derecognition procedures for missing PPEs and for accounting records as stipulated in Sections 7 and 8 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to establish accurate PPE balances.
We also recommended that the City Mayor require the Inventory Committee to validate the existence and condition of the 21 service vehicles and provide for the valuation of such PPEs for the recording by the Accounting Office, if warranted.
observations.managementAction:
The City Accountant validated the existence and condition of the 21 service vehicles and the City GSO started the preparation of Report on Local Road Network and the Road Map. Out of the 21 service vehicles, 10 were recorded in the books. Upon validation, 10 vehicles were not warranted while the last one was still subject for verification as the document found was not in the name of the City.
The City GSO is committed in completing the one-time cleansing process pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006 resolving the unreconciled balances between the RPCPPE and accounting records, ensuring accurate financial reporting and asset management.
observations.reason: However, the reconciliation of inventory count per RPCPPE with accounting records were not yet done. For preparation of Recommendation Tracking Sheet (RTS) for recommendations considered closed for having reiterated with modifications.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor require the Chairman of the Committee on Disposal to initiate/facilitate the disposal of the subject properties, in accordance with the afore-cited provisions and with the Revised Manual on the Disposal of Government Properties and provide the necessary adjusting entries in the books of accounts.
observations.managementAction: However, the City was not able to prioritize the disposal of unserviceable properties. To address the issue, the City Mayor in January 20, 2025, issued Administrative Order (AO) No. 01 series of 2025 to reorganize the City Inventory and Disposal Committee, in compliance with COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2024-1. The Chairman of the Disposal Committee disclosed that they aim to dispose all unserviceable properties this year. Execution of this process is expected to be completed by the second quarter of CY 2025.
observations.reason: However, the City was not able to initiate the disposal process within the prescribed timeframe, they have scheduled a meeting with the relevant Disposal Committee members to ensure that the necessary actions are taken.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to adjust and withhold taxes from Accounts Payable still outstanding as of date, representing procured goods and services, and apply this practice prospectively in succeeding accounting periods, pursuant to BIR Memorandum Circular No. 10-2018.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant commented that they shall adhere with the recommendation to adjust and withhold taxes from Accounts Payable still outstanding as of date and apply this practice prospectively in succeeding accounting periods, pursuant to BIR Memorandum Circular No. 10-2018.
observations.reason: The non-withholding of taxes for the recognized payables was due to delay in the submission of supporting documents by the concerned department which will serve as their basis in the recognition of Due to BIR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor to: a. prepare the GAD Agenda in accordance with the mandate of 4.C.2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 and Sections 1 and 3 of the PCW Memorandum Circular 2018-04; b. enhance their knowledge and build their competencies on GAD-related laws and capability programs; and c. monitor the implementation of the GAD Agenda according to the budget allotted for the year to avoid over/under utilization of the funds intended for each specific programs and activities.
observations.managementAction: The City’s GFPS through the City Planning and Development Office (CPDO), spearheads the formulation and implementation of GAD related plans and programs. The CPDO GAD Unit, unfortunately due to its limited personnel, unsuccessfully met the required document. Despite the circumstance, the GFPS had laid down gender-responsive programs and projects in response to existing gender issues. The GFPS initiated efforts in crafting the GAD Agenda on 2024. There was an orientation and workshop attended by the GFPS members last November 2024 where the City’s GAD Mission and Vision was crafted which is aligned with the identified gender-related issues and concerns. The GFPS has included in its GPB 2025 the hiring of additional staff who will work solely in addressing identified GAD-related concerns.
observations.reason: The CPDO GAD Unit due to limited personnel unsuccessfully met the required document. The GFPS had laid down programs but the GAD Agenda consolidation was not done.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a. City Health Officer (CHO) to obtain from DOH Central Office, the actual information on the unit cost and quantities of the COVID-19 vaccines by the City; and render a monthly report to the City Accounting Office on the receipt and issuance and balances of vaccines or other medicines/supplies received from outside source; b. City Accountant to coordinate with the CHO to determine and recognize in the books the full cost of the ending inventory of vaccines as at year-end and to record future receipts and issuances of the same.
observations.managementAction: In March of 2023, the City Health Office sent a letter to the DOH Regional Office to inquire on the unit cost of the COVID-19 vaccines, however due to Non-Disclosure Agreement signed by the DOH, the unit cost were not provided. No monthly report on receipt, issuance and balances of vaccines or other medicines/supplies was forwarded to the Accounting Office. The City is still in the process and has not yet made the necessary entries due to non-provision of necessary reports by the CHO.
observations.reason: No action has been taken due to non-provision of necessary reports by the CHO.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Solid Waste Management Board to assist all barangays in the reestablishment of an individual or common Materials Recovery Facility (MRF) and ensure that the City’s MRF is existing and fully operational.
observations.managementAction: The City Solid Waste Management Board held a review and updated its Ten-Year Solid Waste Management Plan and started actions for MRF establishment. Out of 30 barangays, 26 barangays have operational MRF; the Office of the Environment and Natural Resources is in coordination with remaining barangays for establishment.
observations.reason: The City has still not established its own sanitary landfill due to budgetary constraints and unavailability of lot suitable for such project.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a. exert additional efforts in the collections from delinquent water consumers; and b. initiate necessary legal actions through the City’s Legal Officer against defaulting consumers in order to enforce collection of long overdue receivables.
observations.managementAction: Collection efforts were exerted by the Management; the City was able to collect P2,174,762.28. The City Treasurer committed to collect remaining delinquent amounts by issuing notices to consumers. Assistance from the head of waterworks and Legal Officer was sought.
observations.reason: Collection efforts are ongoing; accounts remained outstanding.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Solid Waste Management Board assist barangays in reestablishment of their MRF and ensure operation of MRFs and that the City consider establishing a sanitary landfill (including preparing Ten-Year Solid Waste Management Plan and Waste Analysis Characterization Study (WACS)).
observations.managementAction: The City Solid Waste Management Board had its first quarterly meeting and updated Ten-Year Solid Waste Management Plan. Out of 30 barangays, 26 have operational MRFs and Office of Environment and Natural Resources is coordinating with the remaining four barangays. The City Solid Waste Management Board is conducting studies for sanitary landfill but not yet implemented due to budget constraints and site availability.
observations.reason: The City has still not prepared a plan for its own sanitary landfill this year due to budgetary constraints and unavailability of lot suitable for this kind of project.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the BAC, thru its Secretariat, to ensure compliance with Section 12.2 of RA No. 9184 and GPPB Resolution No. 39-2017 dated December 21, 2017 and GPPB Circular No. 02-2020 dated May 20, 2020 in the posting and submission of APP, PMR and APCPI to promote transparency in procurement.
observations.managementAction: The City Government has still not prepared the PMRs and APCPI; Management did not prioritize the preparation. Meeting was conducted and actions to accelerate process discussed.
observations.reason: Management did not prioritize preparation hence posting requirement was not yet implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended City Mayor require City GSO to identify insurable properties and submit consolidated PIF to GSIS and Audit Team; City Budget Officer to appropriate funds for premiums; and insure properties accordingly.
observations.managementAction: City GSO will prepare PIF. Prioritization plan and budget will be proposed. Review of budget was initiated. Some properties insured; not yet all insured.
observations.reason: Not all properties were insured by year-end. The CGSO and Property Custodian still failed to prepare and submit the Consolidated PIF.

observations.recommendation: We recommended City GSO identify insurable properties, submit Consolidated PIF to GSIS and Audit Team, include premiums in annual budget and insure with GSIS.
observations.managementAction: City attempting to prepare consolidated PIF; some properties insured; prioritization and budget proposal planned. COA recommendations reiterated.
observations.reason: Not all serviceable properties were insured; PIF not completed and submitted; planning and budgeting for premiums not yet fully implemented.

observations.recommendation:
1.15 We recommended that the LCE direct the:
a. ME to conduct a mandatory year-end inspection of all ongoing projects and prepare duly certified inspection reports to support the reported percentage of completion in the Status Report on Quarterly PPAs, to serve as basis for the MA to recognize the corresponding accruals in the books;
b. MBO to update the SAAOb and ensure that all obligations for the year are reflected therein; and
c. MA to ensure that the total costs incurred for all PPAs are properly accrued and that obligations are fully and accurately recognized in the SCBAA.

2.6 We recommended that the LCE instruct the:
a. MBO, in coordination with the concerned departments, to carefully plan and assess the anticipated expenditures associated with their planned programs/ projects/ activities (PPAs) to prevent, or at least minimize, instances of insufficient or depleted budgets; and
b. MA to ensure that all transactions are properly recorded under their appropriate accounts, regardless of the budget source, to accurately reflect the nature of expenditures and ensure the reliability of information presented in the financial statements.

3.9 We recommended that the LCE instruct the MA to:
a. Conduct a thorough review and analysis of all accounts by exerting diligent efforts to locate all necessary supporting documents essential for verifying the existence and validity of long outstanding and dormant accounts; and
b. Subsequently, prepare the required schedules and formally request for approval from the COA to write off or derecognize dormant accounts from the books in accordance with the procedures prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005.

4.7 We recommended that the LCE direct the MA to:
a. Investigate and trace the unreconciled balance of ₱14,507,856.99 and make necessary adjustments to the Statement of Cash Flows to reflect accurate net cashflows from operating expenses; and
b. Ensure consistency of reconciling items with related accounts in the Statements of Financial Position and Financial Performance and enhance controls over reconciliation processes to prevent future inconsistencies.

5.7 We recommended that the LCE direct the MA and MT to adopt the ATM payroll system for the payment of salaries, allowances and benefits in compliance with Section 3 of EO No. 170 s. 2022 to promote operational efficiency and strengthen internal controls over payroll system management.

6.15 We recommended that the LCE direct the MT to:
a. Regularly update the cashbook and reconcile it with the actual cash balance on a daily basis to promptly detect and prevent any errors or discrepancies, and to ensure timely corrective actions for effective cash monitoring and control; and
b. Discontinue the practice of using daily collections to temporarily augment funds for the AICS in order to prevent potential cash shortages.

7.8 We recommended that the LCE direct the Petty Cash Custodian/Acting GSO to:
a. refrain from charging regular expenses to the PCF and strictly utilize the fund for non-recurring and petty or miscellaneous expenses in accordance with Section 84, Chapter 3 of GAM for LGUs Vol. I; and
b. make proactive efforts to identify other eligible gasoline stations within the locality, or at least in adjacent municipalities, that are willing to supply fuel and enter into a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) with the Municipality.

7.9 We further recommended that the LCE instruct the requisitioning offices to establish a system for regular inventory monitoring and to promptly initiate supplies requisition once predetermined threshold levels are reached to prevent depletion.

8.8 We recommended that the LCE direct the AAO to:
a. Ensure strict compliance with Section 56 of RA No. 11975 by closely monitoring payroll deductions which can be achieved by maintaining individual ledgers per employee which will serve as a basis for evaluating and approving future loan applications to help prevent employees' NTPH from falling below the required minimum threshold and safeguard their financial well-being;
b. Formulate internal policies and procedures governing the evaluation and approval of employees’ salary loan applications. These should include a clear provision requiring the certification of NTHP as a mandatory requirement in the evaluation and approval of loans. Once drafted, it should be submitted to the LCE for review and approval; and
c. Refrain from issuing or signing certifications for loan applications in cases where an employee’s NTHP would fall below the minimum threshold of ₱5,000.00, exclusive of other loan obligations.

8.9 We also recommended the Human Resource and Development Officer to conduct extensive information and awareness campaigns for all employees to ensure a comprehensive understanding of loan-related policies and requirements to empower employees with essential knowledge and promote financial literacy within the organization.

9.11 We recommended that the LCE direct the GAD Focal Person to:
a. Institutionalize a more rigorous evaluation process for proposed GAD PPAs by requiring the use of gender analysis tools such as the HGDG and ensuring alignment with documented gender issues, as required under Sections C.1.4 and C.4 of the Circular. Proposals that do not address gender gaps should be revised or excluded from the GAD Plan; and
b. Strengthen the functionality of the GFPS by providing adequate support and capacity-building, and by establishing internal mechanisms to harmonize interdepartmental proposals with GAD mandates—ensuring that only compliant and gender-responsive programs are included in the final Plan.

11.9 We recommended that the LCE direct the MBO to:
a. Strictly comply with the provisions of LBM No. 88 by ensuring that any proposed utilization of savings from prior years' excise tax collections is first submitted to the DBM for the required prior approval before the commencement or implementation of any project to uphold fiscal discipline, ensure proper authorization, and maintain accountability in the use of public funds; and
b. Apprise himself of the pertinent rules and regulations governing the use of RA No. 7171 and RA No. 8240 funds, including the provisions of Local Budget Memoranda and other relevant issuances, to ensure proper fund utilization in accordance with applicable budgeting laws and guidelines.

12.7 We recommended the LCE to direct the MT to:
a. Revisit and review the terms of the existing lease agreements, and incorporate specific provisions that address known concerns of lessees and anticipated operational issues, ensuring that such revisions do not compromise the public interest and continue to uphold accountability and revenue generation for the Municipality;
b. Issue written notices to all delinquent lessees, directing them to settle their outstanding accounts immediately, and if necessary, initiate the termination of their lease contracts; and
c. Henceforth, strictly enforce the terms and conditions of the lease agreements to ensure compliance by all lessees and uphold the integrity of contractual obligations.

13.7 We recommended the LCE to direct the MT to:
a. Furnish the MA with copies of all lease contracts to facilitate the proper preparation and maintenance of lease monitoring records; and
b. In consonance with the initial recommendation under the second audit observation, incorporate a provision for interest charges on delayed rental payments to ensure timely collection and discourage delinquency.

13.8 We also recommended the LCE to direct the MA to:
a. Prepare and maintain a lease monitoring schedule in accordance with the terms and conditions stipulated in the lease contracts to be provided by the MT;
b. Compute the interest due from delinquent lessees using the legal interest rate of 6% per annum, in the absence of a stipulated rate, in accordance with Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) Circular No. 799 dated July 1, 2013, and subsequently inform all concerned lessees in writing to settle their interest obligations;
c. Restate the affected income accounts to reflect accurate figures in the preparation of the CY 2025 financial statements; and
d. Henceforward, record accrued income in the books to accurately reflect the Municipality’s true financial performance.

15.10 We recommended that the LCE direct the BAC to:
a. Use the updated APP template from the GPPB's official website, which specifies the applicable alternative mode of procurement; and
b. If the original mode of procurement cannot be pursued, ensure that a BAC Resolution is prepared, recommending and justifying the changes to the HoPE, in accordance with Section 48.3 of the Revised IRR.

16.8 We recommended the LCE to direct the:
a. Department heads to identify the projects, programs and activities in their respective departments that require meals and snacks; and
b. BAC to consolidate the requirements of all the departments and procure the Municipality’s meals and snacks through competitive bidding adopting the Framework Agreement.

17.8 We recommended the LCE to instruct all end-user units to provide detailed technical specifications for all requested items moving forward, to ensure clarity, facilitate proper procurement planning, and promote fair and competitive bidding.

18.21 We recommended that the LCE direct the MSWDO to:
a. Prepare and submit a comprehensive list of positions included in the Plantilla of Personnel, along with the corresponding job descriptions or actual duties, specifically indicating the nature and extent of occupational risks involved in the performance of each position. This list shall serve as the basis for justifying eligibility to receive HP and other compensation-related benefits, and must be duly reviewed and approved by the LCE; and
b. Secure the required DSWD certification, following the procedures set forth in DSWD MC No. 5, series of 2022, to officially confirm the designation of personnel as PSWs, which shall serve as the basis of their entitlement to HP and other compensation-related benefits.

18.22 We also recommended that the LCE direct the MA to ensure that all claims for HP of Social Workers are supported by documentation proving actual service rendered during the claimed period and see to it that the same be computed in accordance with Section 16 of the IRR of RA No. 9433.

18.23 Furthermore, we recommended the LCE direct the persons liable to refund the overpayment amounting to ₱36,277.15.

19.11 We recommended that the LCE direct the BAC to:
a. Conduct a thorough assessment of the sufficiency of the contractor’s manpower across all awarded projects, especially those with overlapping implementation periods under the same contractor, and require the contractor to take appropriate action to address any identified deficiencies in manpower utilization;
b. Ensure that the post-qualification process involves comprehensive verification, validation, and confirmation of all statements and documents submitted by the bidder with the Lowest Calculated Bid. The Post-Qualification Report should clearly reflect detailed findings, particularly with regard to the verification of technical requirements; and
c. Include in the Special Conditions of Contract a provision requiring that any bidder, whether intending to participate in multiple contracts or currently holding an on-going contract/project with the municipality should allocate separate and dedicated manpower for each project.

19.20 We recommended the LCE to direct the BAC to scrutinize, during bid evaluation, the submitted financial documents of the contractor, particularly its detailed estimates, to conform with the limitations imposed in the ABC and DO.

19.29 We recommended the LCE to direct the BAC Secretariat to post the NOA, NTP, and the approved contracts on the PhilGEPs website within the prescribed period, as required by Sections 37.1.6 and 37.4.2 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

19.36 We recommended and Management approved to direct the BAC and its TWG to require the winning bidder to submit a CSHP duly approved by the DOLE before the signing of any infrastructure contract in accordance with Section 37.2 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

19.37 Likewise, we recommended and Management also agreed to direct the BAC Secretariat to submit to the AT the DOLE Certifications for the remaining 16 contracts, for verification.

We have recommended that Management undertake the following courses of action:
Department heads dealing with multiple responsibilities may appoint a capable staff member as their alternate to handle the necessary tasks required to address issues in road networks.
The Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant must locate the missing records and provide the necessary information in the RPCLRN; and
The General Services Officer or its equivalent has to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components using the prescribed format under COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015.

We have recommended that Management:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to revert the unutilized balance of the LDRRMF, pertaining to the QRF and the DRRMF-MOOE, to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund after five years, and shall be made available for other social services after subsequent enactment by the local sanggunian; and
Determine whether the projects related to the unutilized Mitigation Fund-Capital Outlay allocated for the years 2014 to 2022, with a total accumulated amount of  ₱2,931,706.72, have been fully implemented, and utilize any savings for disaster risk reduction and management activities as provided in the LDRRMFIP.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Engineer, in collaboration with the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and Technical Working Group (TWG), to submit the necessary documents to the STSS for a comprehensive technical evaluation and inspection to ensure compliance with design specifications and construction standards.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive, who has direct responsibility for the installation, implementation, and monitoring of an effective internal control system within the LGU, enforce that all payments for the procurement of goods and services be made by check directly to suppliers, creditors, or dealers. This will ensure that the corresponding tax is withheld in accordance with applicable laws, rules, and regulations.
Furthermore, we suggest that management consider the following courses of action to tackle the previously mentioned issue:
Ensure that all procurements of the LGU are meticulously and judiciously planned and within the approved budget of the Procuring Entity; and
Require check payments to suppliers except for expenses that are petty in nature, non-recurring, and urgently needed, which shall be charged out of the PCF.

We have recommended that Management require the BAC to:
Discontinue immediately the practice of procuring supplies and/or items from non-PhilGEPS-registered suppliers.
Ensure that only qualified suppliers are requested to tender their price quotations and/or awarded contracts in compliance with Section 8.5.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and Section J of the Guidelines on Shopping and Small Value Procurement.

We have recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to:
Monitor the balances of appropriations and obligations regularly, through the RAO, to ensure the availability of an appropriation as certified in the CAFOA; and
Secure proper authorization of the augmentation of funds prior to its utilization to ensure that all expenditures are paid pursuant to an appropriation ordinance or law. Otherwise, the disbursement is unauthorized, hence disallowable in audit.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that Management require the BAC Chairperson, through the BAC Secretariat, to:
Prepare all the documents required under each procurement stage on time to ensure that all documents are completed at the end of each procurement process; and
Submit copies of perfected contracts, approved POs, and supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within the prescribed period of five (5) working days pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 for a timely review and evaluation. Administrative disciplinary action, as provided in Section 4 of the same circular, against the persons responsible, shall be imposed for unjustified failure to comply.

We have recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, to undertake the following courses of action, to wit:
Ensure that supporting documents of DVs are complete and duly signed before processing the payment;
Provide proof of notifications sent to suppliers and creditors about checks claimed between one month and four months to explain the cause of the delay issued by the Treasury Office concerning the submission of accounts; and
Consider establishing written agreements with suppliers/payees authorizing the deposit of checks into the payee's account if they remain unclaimed by the end of the month, provided that the claim does not require the issuance of official receipts. Then, attach the deposit slip to the disbursement voucher as evidence of its deposit into the payee’s account.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Chairperson of the BAC require the Municipal Accountant to:
Oversee its members as the head of the TWG to thoroughly verify the completeness of the PO during the procurement process; and
Make sure all documents are complete when processing the payment for goods, including the validity of the POs.

To address the foregoing issues, we have strongly recommended that Management undertake the following courses of action:
Consider adopting electronic or computerized systems for accounting, budgeting, and collections, as they would result in timely and cost-efficient transaction processing. Automating routine transactions would significantly reduce the workload of municipal heads and staff, promote efficiency, and enable the timely and accurate generation and submission of reports.
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to facilitate the implementation of the ATM or digitized payroll system for disbursing salaries, wages, and other emoluments to elected officials and both regular and casual employees of the LGU. This system enhances internal cash control, improves service delivery to employees, and reduces the workload and accountability of the Municipal Treasurer, ensuring a more efficient and secure payroll process.
In light of the advantages of computerized systems for processing government financial transactions and digitized payroll systems for disbursing salaries and employee benefits, the implementation of the aforementioned audit recommendations is strongly encouraged.

We have strongly recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer to adopt the ATM or digitized payroll system in the disbursement of payroll for salaries, wages and other emoluments of elected officials, regular and casual employees of the LGU. The adoption of a digitized payroll system is highly encouraged because it strengthens internal control on cash with improved delivery service to employees at reduced costs.

We have recommended that Management designate a member of the LSB to oversee and monitor the submission and receipt of quarterly and annual reports on the SEF budget to the concerned offices and oversight agencies to ensure timely submission.

We have recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to:
Enforce the submission of BIR-validated ITR from business taxpayers; and
Reassess taxes, including past years' business taxes, using the BIR-validated ITRs to recover unpaid business taxes, including applicable interest and surcharges pursuant to Section 2C.04, Paragraph (e) of the updated Municipal Revenue Code.
For the LGU to lawfully enforce compliance and deter non-submission of ITRs, we further recommended that Management:
Ensure that the Municipality of Sto. Tomas has a codified ordinance specifying fines, permit suspensions, or revocation for failure to submit required documents;
When the required ITR is not submitted by the taxpayer,  a formal notice will be sent to the non-compliant business, citing the specific violation (e.g., failure to submit ITR) and referencing the Municipal Revenue Code, allowing 15 calendar days to submit the ITR or respond; and
For the renewal of business permits, which takes place from January 1 to 20 each year, in addition to the Sworn Statement of Gross Receipts or Sales for the initial computation of business taxes of the current year, the submission of the previous year's ITR be required for further reassessment if the last payment of business tax was based on certification or self-declared income, otherwise, the issuance of business permit be temporarily suspended until the required documents has been complied with.

To address the foregoing issues, we have reiterated the prior years’ audit recommendations that Management consider preparing an action plan where different strategies could be applied based on the age and nature of the collectibles and realistically project collections under the circumstances. The following collection strategies may be considered:
Prioritize intensified tax mapping in the first four barangays with the highest delinquent accounts;
Advise the Chief of RPTS to seek further assistance and intervention of barangay officials in the collection of delinquent accounts; and
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to pursue administrative and judicial measures after all other collection strategies have been exhausted without success.

2.22. We restated our prior years' recommendation and supplemented additions thereto and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct:
(a) the Municipal Accountant to (i) ensure that all purchases of supplies and materials are recorded under the proper inventory account; (ii) prepare supplies ledger cards for delivered items as called for in the above-quoted regulation and value inventories using moving average method;
(b) the BAC and the Acting Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere to the requirements provided in COA Circular Nos. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 and 2023-004 dated June 14, 2023, before payment thereof; and
(c) the GSO to (i) prepare and maintain stock cards for all category of purchased supplies and materials; (ii) consolidate all purchase requests pertaining to the procurement of supplies and submit the consolidated request to the Bids and Awards Committee for centralized procurement to avail of the quantity discount inherent to bulk orders and procure, as far as practicable, directly from the PS-DBM all anticipated common-use supplies and equipment, and resort to local suppliers only upon unavailability of supplies needed; and
(d) the Inventory Committee to conduct semestral physical count of inventories, to detect discrepancies, and to safeguard the inventories of the Municipality.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the concerned Officials and employees
to complete the procedures of one-time cleansing, pursuant to COA Circular No.
2020-006, as follows:

a. The Property Unit:
• in coordination with the City Inventory and Disposal Committee, perform the
 disposition procedures for non-existing/missing PPEs such as sending of
demand letters to the last known custodians. On the other hand, for non-
existing/missing PPEs for which accountability could not be established,
cause the conduct of an investigation to determine the last known
location/office;
• furnish the Accounting Division of necessary documents such as Inventory and
Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property, approved demolition permit and
documentation of demolition for dropping of records in the books of accounts the
demolished buildings. In case of no available records, follow the procedures in
the disposition of non-existing/missing PPEs in Sections 7 and 8;

b. The Accounting Division to:
• to set-up receivable accounts from the personnel who are unable to produce the
PPE items;
• consider requesting authority for derecognition from the books of accounts from
the COA, if accountability/responsibility over the missing PPE could not be
pinpointed after investigation, in accordance with Section 8;

c. The Property and Accounting Division to:
• complete the disposition for non-existing/missing PPEs and the derecognition of
non-existing/missing PPEs without available record of accountability as stated in
Sections 7 and 8; and
• complete the reconciliation procedures, wherein the total of the reconciled
balances for each PPE account shall be established as the correct balance of
PPE of the government agency as of the end of the year.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Administrator, in coordination
with the City Accountant, to set cut-off date on the submission of supporting
documents for Accounts Payable at year-end to the Office of the City Accountant and
monitor the compliance of all Department Heads of the City. Likewise, we
recommended that the City Accountant ensure complete documentation before
recording of Accounts Payable.

Furthermore, we reiterated that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to adjust
and withhold taxes from Accounts Payable still outstanding as of date, representing
procured goods and services, and apply this practice prospectively in the succeeding
periods, pursuant to BIR Memorandum Circular No. 10-2018.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to:
a. Initiate the adoption of policy providing specific estimated useful lives based
on the experience of the agency with similar assets. Otherwise, adopt the useful
lives provided under COA Circular No. 2003-007 dated December 11, 2003;
b. Recompute the depreciation based on the approved estimated useful life of
each asset; and
c. Prepare the appropriate journal entries and other accounting records to
correct the depreciation and effect the necessary adjustments in order to
rectify the errors and present the asset accounts to their proper carrying
amounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
a. Review the validity of Guaranty/Security Deposits Payable which are
outstanding for over one year; and
b. Notify the contractors and private entities concerned to file their claims for the
immediate release of the retention money to avoid accumulation of the
account balances in the books. Otherwise, require them to execute a waiver of
their right to claim such deposits for reversion to the unappropriated surplus
of the general fund.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:

a. City General Services Officer to prepare the PIF by extracting the data from the
RPCPPE and submit the same to the Audit Team and GIF of the GSIS which
will be used as a basis for the assessment of the general insurance coverage
of all the City’s insurable properties;
b. City Budget Officer to include in the City’s annual budget the amount of
premiums for the general insurance covering all insurable properties, pursuant to
COA Circular No. 2018-002; and
c. City General Services Officer to insure with the GIF of the GSIS all of the City’s
insurable assets and properties with the appropriate insurance coverage,
pursuant to RA No. 656 and COA Circular No. 2018-002.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Human Resource and Management
Officer (HRMO), in coordination with the City Budget Officer, to review the existing
policy on the hiring and compensation of JO personnel and ensure that payment is in
accordance at least with the latest minimum wage rate issued by the DOLE, subject to
City’s financial capacity, or if funds are available, a comparable rate with the regular
employees and similar items or positions having similar rate for fair and equitable
compensation.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Procurement Head to strictly
adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-001 on the prompt submission of
copies of perfected Contracts and POs and their supporting documents within the
reglementary period of five working days after execution to facilitate timely
review/evaluation of the legal and technical aspects of the contracts, and for
Management to be informed early of any deficiencies noted in audit.

We reiterated that the City Mayor require the Disposal Committee to:

a. Coordinate with the Appraisal Committee to determine the appraised values of
the unserviceable properties; and
b. Fast track the disposal of the unserviceable PPE items costing P48,607,176.62
to prevent further deterioration of the assets and decongest the storage area,
in accordance with the procedures provided by the Revised Manual on
Disposal of Government Properties.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. Require the City Procurement Head to coordinate with the suppliers for the
submission of brochures/manuals and Performance/Quality Test Results of the
six procurement projects, if applicable;
b. Instruct the BAC Chairman to include in the requirements of prospective
bidders/suppliers the brochures/manuals of goods to be supplied as well as
the Performance/Quality Test Results, if applicable, in compliance with COA
Circular No. 2009-001; and
c. Require the end-users to refrain from specifying brand names or technical
specifications tailor-fitting to a particular brand/product, pursuant to Section
18 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We reiterated that the City Mayor as the Chairperson of the Executive Committee to
require the GAD Focal Point System and the Technical Working Group to:

a. Prepare the GAD Agenda as soon as possible and strictly comply with the
provisions on the preparation of the GAD Agenda to ensure that the PPAs
included in the annual GPB are aligned with it or with the identified gender
issues to be addressed during the three-year term of the LCE; and
b. Strictly monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs and conduct regular
coordination with the implementing offices to ensure that programs are
undertaken within the target period.

observations.managementAction:
The ME already determined the actual completion dates of the LRN and forwarded his report to the MA for review and recording.
The MA had already computed the depreciation related thereto and made the necessary adjusting entries to correct the amount presented in prior period adjustments as reflected under JEV no. 2024-100-10-6050 dated December 27, 2024

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE direct both the MT-Designate and the MA to:
a. submit the Deeds of Donation evidencing the distribution of the 26 hand tractors and 2 water pumps; b. use the RIS as a supporting document in the distribution of supplies; c. provide all the necessary details of the distribution list; d. prepare required documents and maintain updated inventory records based on the receipts, acceptance, and issuance of inventory items as prescribed by the Manual; e. strictly follow the procedures set forth in the Manual; and f. conduct semestral (or oftener depending on the discretion of management) physical count of inventory items to be reported in the PRCI which should be submitted to the Audit Team’s Office.
We further recommended that the LCE direct the MA to record the following adjusting journal entries:
observations.managementAction:
The concerned office is currently in the process of preparing and complying the required documents.
The Municipality has prepared a detailed distribution list
The Municipality is in the process of complying with the recommendation.
The Municipality is in the process of complying with the recommendation.
The Municipality is in the process of complying with the recommendation.
The MA had already recorded the necessary adjusting entry under JEV no. 2024-100-04-1802 dated April 29, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the following: a. The MA and the OIC-MT to stop the practice of withholding business taxes from payments to suppliers since these two are different and distinct from one another; b. The BPLO to send Notices of Assessment to the business address of the contractors; and c. The MA to: i. record the advance collection of business tax as credit to Other Deferred Credits account; and ii. make the following adjusting entry: Business Tax ₱246,136.86 Other Deferred Credits ₱246,136.86
observations.managementAction:
The MA and the MT stopped this practice per recommendation of the COA.
The BPLO had already sent Notices of Assessment to the contractors; and provided the BPLO EE Madayag Construction and JA Olivar Engineering Design Construction and supplies.
The MA effected the necessary adjustment on the records. JEV No. 2024-100-04-1803 has been drawn by the MA to record the advance collection of business tax and correct the amount of business taxes for CY 2023 reported in the financial statements.
JEV No. 2024-100-04-1802 has been drawn by the MA to record the advance collection of business tax and correct the amount of business taxes for CY 2023 reported in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the LCE direct the following: a. The Sangguniang Bayan Office to: i. use resources carefully and deliberately, ensuring that they are utilized only when necessary and in appropriate quantities; ii. procure items that the office can actually use by aligning them with their APP; and b. The Inspection Committee and General Services Officer to centralize the management practices of the Sangguniang Bayan's office and janitorial supplies requests in accordance with the inventory management practices of the executive department.
observations.managementAction:
The SB Office requested the needed supplies based on the availability and evaluation through their APP.
The IC and GSO strictly followed the inventory centralized management practices of the executive department.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the following: a. The Municipal Health Officer to incorporate the prices indicated in the DPRI into the purchase requests per line item of the medicine; and b. The BAC and the Head of the Technical Working Group, to review the prices of medicines indicated on the purchase requests and in any case where the latest DPRI prices published has no reference price available for certain essential medicines, they may resort to previous year’s awarded price as an Approved Budget for the Contract or may conduct a market survey and use the cheapest price in the market.
observations.managementAction:
The MHO prepared a PR to request the necessary medicines based on the DPRI.
The BAC and TWG used the DPRI, or where DPRI has no reference price, they used previous awarded price or conducted market survey.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the following: The OIC-Municipal Agriculture to: i. present a thorough and detailed project proposal for initiatives involving the distribution of assistance to farmers, with a focus on identifying target beneficiaries (pre-master list) and establishing a clear distribution schedule; ii. utilize the Registry System for Basic Sectors in Agriculture for future projects involving farmers in the identification of target beneficiaries; iii. procure/request items based on the needs of farmers; and iv. actively participate on the distribution process and see to it that qualified beneficiaries received the items. We further recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee, considering the warehousing of goods and the risks accompanying it, to utilize Framework Agreement in the procurement of goods which are repeatedly required but by their nature, use, or characteristic, the quantity or exact time of need cannot be accurately predetermined; and not advisable to be carried in stock.
observations.managementAction: The concerned office submitted the detailed project proposal. The Municipality prepared a detailed project proposal. The Municipality is in the process of complying with the recommendation. The project implementer was actively involved in the distribution process. A temporary storage facility was designated to house the delivered items. A framework agreement was utilized for warehousing and procurement as needed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the BAC to: a. use the updated form of contract and financial bid form; b. ensure that the contracts were notarized; c. ensure that all the procurement documents were signed; and d. verify the veracity of the documents submitted by the bidder/s before entering into the contract and ensure that the CSHP is secured first by the winning bidder prior to award and start of contract implementation. Furthermore, for the BAC to keep themselves updated on the latest issuances of the GPPB.
observations.managementAction: The BAC has already complied. The MA had confirmed that she is already requiring surety bonds worth 30% of the contract price from suppliers/contractors. Relevant documents were notarized and signed in subsequent contracts. Some CSHP submissions were still delayed after contract signing and management committed to require DOLE certifications prior to contract signing in future.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the following: a. The MA and the MT to submit the following reports financial data to the Local School Board on or before July 15: i. actual SEF Income for the immediately preceding fiscal year; ii. actual SEF Income for the first two quarters of the current year; iii. estimated SEF Income for the last two quarters of the current year; and iv. annual Estimates for the ensuing year. b. The Local School Board to consider: i. appropriating the previous year’s unexpended balances in formulating the current year’s budget and; ii. reallocating the continuing appropriations mentioned above if these are no longer needed and using them to fund the other requests proposed by schools, provided that these requests are in line with the DEPED, DBM and DILG JC No. 1, 2017.
observations.managementAction: The enumerated reports were submitted to the LSB. The unexpended balances were included in the current year budget. The LSB included the unexpended balance ₱6,965,387.53 in the 2025 budget.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the LSB to: a. formulate the budget on a per-school allocation or per-activity basis to support the provisions set forth by the JC; and b. conduct DEPED classroom deployment analysis before formulating the budget, specifically on the capital outlay projects.
observations.managementAction: The LSB budget for the current year was allocated on a per-activity basis. The LSB conducted an FGD with the school heads to determine the felt needs of their respective schools.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the following: a. The MA to: i. require the school heads to submit the liquidation report of the granted financial assistance amounting to ₱353,000.00; and ii. in the case of financial assistance to athletes and other activities, require a detailed breakdown of possible expenses on the request letter; and b. The Bids and Awards Committee to procure items to be used by various schools based on the assigned budget in accordance with applicable laws, rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: Liquidation report already submitted to COA. BAC already procured items needed by the schools based on assigned budget.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the following: a. The Sanggunian Bayan (SB) to expedite the resolution approval process of the SB Resolution regarding the establishment of the petty cash fund; b. The OIC-MT, upon the approval of the SB Resolution, to establish petty cash fund; and c. The MA to: i. cease processing vouchers for minor expenses, such as, but not limited to meals and snacks, repairs and maintenance, office supplies, and miscellaneous expenses, in the form of reimbursements; ii. verify the RERs in alignment with COA Circular 2021-001; iii. closely oversee the validation of transaction documents to ensure compliance with COA Circular 2012-001; and iv. deduct taxes from payments to various suppliers as per BIR Memorandum Order No. 23-2014.
observations.managementAction: The SB passed SB resolution No. 28 Series of 2024 establishing petty cash fund amounting to ₱100,000.00. The MT has established a Petty Cash Fund and started disbursing funds through it. RERs were strictly validated and taxes were deducted where applicable. Some PCF transactions showed regular expenses charged due to operational challenges and PCF usage continues for gasoline until MOA with supplier is in place.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the GAD Focal Chairperson to: a. ensure the optimum utilization of GAD funds through the implementation of all identified programs, projects, and activities to attain the GAD objective of pursuing gender equality and empowerment; b. consider simultaneous implementation of the programs and not relying on the utilization of similar programs first before using the GAD budget; and c. consider reallocating initially planned GAD programs if such programs cannot be pursued due to contingent events.
observations.managementAction: The LCE directed the concerned offices to implement their respective PPAs as scheduled. The LCE directed the MPDO and Budget Office, in coordination with concerned offices, to reallocate funds if no longer implementable. The Municipality implemented all PPAs in its initial GAD Plan.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the following: a. The BPLO to verify the documents presented by the business owners; b. The OIC-MT to assess the business tax or assign her duly authorized representative; and c. The collector should focus on the collection of business taxes.
observations.managementAction: Verification of documents is already being performed by the BPLO. The assessment is being performed by the MT and its authorized representative. The designated collector works full time during the BOSS and focuses on collection rather than assessment.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the: a. MT to inform the MA of the inventories received and issued through submission of delivery receipts, SSMI and RIS to the MA for recording purposes;
observations.managementAction: The MT provided copies of the aforesaid documents to the MA for recording purposes. Likewise, SSMI and RIS were utilized whenever supplies are distributed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the: a. MT 1. To require all collectors to immediately remit to her the year-end collections at the first working day of the ensuing year; 2. To prepare a separate Abstract of Collections for the prior year’s collection receipts from current year’s collections; and 3. To promptly submit the collection receipts and reports to the MA for recording purposes.
observations.managementAction: All collections were remitted by the collectors to the Municipal Treasurer. The Municipal Treasury staff prepared a separate Abstract of Collections for the prior year collections. The Municipal Treasury Office promptly submitted the aforesaid documents to the MA for recording.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that LCE direct the: a. Bids and Awards Committee to be updated on issuances in order to comply with the regulations and avoid sanctions; b. MA in collaboration with the BAC Secretariat, to submit immediately the lacking documentary requirements, and thereafter, ensure submission of complete documentation for all transactions; and c. The officer-in-charge to process immediately the OR-CR of the dump truck.
observations.managementAction:
BAC already acquainted on the latest DBM circular.
Lacking documentary requirements were already submitted to the Audit Team.
Transfer is in process; awaiting the release of documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Head of the Procuring Entity direct the: a. Bids and Awards Committee to: i) remind prospective bidders during the pre-bid conference of the completeness of information in the required Class "A" documents, particularly the completeness of declared ongoing and awarded but not yet started government and private contracts and the accuracy of declared NFCC. Otherwise, disqualify non-compliant bidders during bid evaluation or post qualification based on the grounds set under the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184; iii) require the suppliers or contractors to post warranty security in accordance with Section 62.1 of the same law; and b. TWG to conduct bid evaluation and post bid evaluation diligently in accordance with pertinent provisions of RA 9184 and its IRR, taking into consideration the key personnel assigned to the projects.
observations.managementAction: BAC reminded bidders during pre-bid and required completeness of Class 'A' documents; BAC required surety bonds and warranty securities where applicable; TWG conducted evaluations and post-qualification and committed to stricter reviews.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the MPDC and Local Development Council to consider revisiting the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) of the Municipality for the past years and determining the barangays that least benefited from funding for the implementation of PPAs, and thereafter, consider the inclusion of the proposed projects of such barangays in the next budget year.
observations.managementAction: The LCE directed the MPDC and LDC to prioritize least benefited barangays for inclusion in the next budgeting year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE, in coordination with the Human Resource Office: 1. revisit and study the organizational structure of the municipality, particularly the staffing pattern of all its offices, and consider abolishing items not considered vital, specifically those that have been left vacant for a long period; and 2. fill up all vacant positions that are still considered vital by hiring new employees or expediting the promotion of deserving job-order employees.
observations.managementAction: Organizational structure was revisited and some vital positions were posted to the Civil Service Commission website. Postings were made and some positions were filled; deserving rank-in-file employees were promoted and some Job Orders were absorbed. In 2023, 11 positions were filled followed by 15 positions in 2024, and eight individuals were hired in the first quarter of 2025.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE capacitate the MDRRMO and his staff through seminars like preparation of Business Continuity Plan, Hazardous Materials Management and Management of the Dead and the Missing. We also recommended that the LCE direct the MDRRMO to consult with the PDRRMO on the PPAs planned for the Municipality pertaining to disaster risk reduction management in order that there will be no overlapping of projects.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMO attended trainings and seminars on Business Continuity Plan, Hazardous Materials Management and Management of the Dead and the Missing. The MDRRMO coordinated with the PDRRMO to avoid overlapping projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the MT to: a) collect the business taxes and penalties from two contractors in the aggregate amount of ₱321,654.17.
observations.managementAction: All business taxes were collected from the two contractors. A copy of the notice was provided to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the MT in collaboration with the Business and Permits Licensing Officer to: a. base the assessment of taxes on sworn gross receipts, Internal Tax Revenue, or Financial Statements; b. conduct regular inspection and verification of the financial records of the business establishments to ensure that the correct gross receipts will be declared by the taxpayer; and c. collect from the taxpayers the under-collection of ₱62,488.67 with corresponding interest, and surcharges, and re-assess all the business taxes for probable tax deficiencies.
observations.managementAction: Assessments were based on sworn gross receipts, ITR or FS. Regular inspections and verifications were conducted; some under-collection was collected (₱40,104.82 out of ₱62,488.67).
observations.reason: Some taxpayers could not be located and some businesses closed; remaining balances uncollected.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct: a. the ME and Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to: 2) recommend re-programming of projects that were not feasible to the Sangguniang Bayan.
observations.managementAction: The LCE directed the Planning and Engineering Offices to reprogram the projects that could no longer be feasible, such as the Construction of Drying Pavement and the Concreting of Farm to Market Road, were reprogrammed to another project—the Construction of the Rock Bulkhead.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipality revert/refund to the 20% Development Fund the amount of ₱233,415.04 pertaining to the penalties incurred due to late payment of loan amortizations and will be subject for re-appropriation to finance development projects.
observations.managementAction: The penalties due to late payment of loan amortizations amounting to ₱233,415.04 was fully settled because this was deducted from the TLB of Mr. Ventenilla prior to his retirement. The said amount was recorded under JEV No. 2023-100-01-48 dated January 9, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We also recommended that the Management ensure that 20% Development Fund be optimally utilized for the attainment of the desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes for the benefit of its constituents.
observations.managementAction: The LCE directed the Planning and the Budget Office to reprogram the 20% Development Fund that were disallowed by the PGLU during the budget review and to utilize the said fund for the benefit of all Tomasians. A review of CY 2024 SAOB disclosed that there were no continuing projects pertaining to 20% DF which means that the disallowed project was reprogrammed and implemented.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the MPDC in coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer to re-appropriate the unexpended balance or savings from the 20% DF to finance the implementation of eligible development projects, thus the utilization of the said funds be optimized for the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environment management outcome of the LGU.
observations.managementAction:
As of this writing, the management submitted to the COA a copy of the appropriation ordinance to reappropriate the unexpended balance or savings from the 20% DF. 
 
The Local Development Council recommended to reappropriate the savings or unexpended balance for the procurement of dump/garbage truck for the collection of solid waste of the entire municipality.
observations.reason: Management immediately addressed the audit issues by complying with the audit recommendations and providing the necessary justifications.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the GAD Focal Person to:

carefully evaluate the suggested and/or attributed PPAs to ensure that these effectively address gender-related issues and concerns; and

shall regularly monitor if the GAD budget is being spent for what it was intended for and check on the actual cost of implementing the GAD Activities.
observations.managementAction:
In the HGDC the calculated score is 4-7.9 and it falls under promising GAD prospects (conditional pass) and enters in Gender- 
Sensitivity where there are 431 female farmers of about 1,026 males with a percentage of 42 % based on the RSBSA data. This FMR program and project is attributed to GAD Program.  
 
In addition to the FMR CCTVs and Riprap are components of the opening and maintenance of Farm-to-market road Program, where the noted variance in GAD actual expenditure (excess over budget) includes the purchase of CCTVs.
observations.reason:
Management immediately addressed the audit issues by complying with the audit recommendations and providing the necessary justifications.
 
Implemented
To date, there was no issue that GAD budget is spent for what was not intended for.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that Management:

Direct the Municipal Accountant to effect immediately the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts amounting to ₱16,578.25 for a fair presentation of balance of the cash in bank and the liability accounts in the financial statements. 



Direct the Municipal Accountant to scrutinize the list of outstanding checks in the preparation of the monthly bank reconciliation statements to determine immediately all checks that become stale to ensure a timely adjustment in the books of accounts.



Instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to be guided by Section 59, Volume 1 of the MNGAS for LGUs relative to the treatment of spoiled and stale checks.
observations.managementAction:
The management notified the payees/ suppliers/creditors of their outstanding cheques to know if they were still interested to claim the amounts payable to them. If they waive their rights on the said claims, adjustments will be taken in the books of accounts in favor of the LGU. 
 
The Accounting Office copy furnished the Treasurer’s Office of the Monthly Bank Reconciliation in order to notify the latter of the “soon-to-be” stale cheques.
observations.reason:
Copies of confirmation letters, acknowledged by the claimants, were provided to the Audit Team. Furthermore, adjustments were made to the books of accounts upon verification of the most recent Bank Reconciliation Statement dated December 31, 2024.

Not Implemented
Based on the Bank Reconciliation as of December 31, 2024, three checks dated July 6, 2023, December 21, 2023, and January 2, 2024, totaling ₱7,000.00 are stale, having been unclaimed for over six months.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that management undertake the following courses of action:

	Require the department heads who are part of the Inventory Committee to designate capable alternates to conduct the physical inventory of road networks.

Require the Municipal Engineer to spearhead the physical inventory of the road networks because of his technical knowledge and skills in engineering.  

	Require the Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects;

Require the General Services Officer or its equivalent to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components using the format prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015; and

Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network using the prescribed format as mentioned in the above circular and disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the total road network system.
observations.managementAction: The management will conduct the necessary physical inventory of all the road networks account which will be spearheaded by Engineer Eugene Garcia. The committee will start with all the Road Projects that Engineer Garcia handled, monitored and documented from the time he was appointed as the municipal engineer of the LGU of Santo Tomas. In addition, the committee will inquire to the previous municipal engineer and engineering staffs for all the Road projects completed before 2016. The Engineering Office was also preparing the Road Map to easily identify and locate each road network constructed from the year 2016 up to the present.
observations.reason:
Capable alternates were designated to conduct the physical inventory of road networks.
 
Implemented
Per EO No. 07, S. 2023, the Municipal Engineer was recognized as one of the Municipal Property Inventory, Appraisal and Disposal Committee.  

Not Implemented

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that Management:

Verify the validity of payables aged two years and more, send confirmation letters for balances with identified claimants or creditors, and remunerate all valid claims. Otherwise, cause the reversion of the payables to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund; 

Exert effort to retrieve the documents of unidentified Other Payables or those without details in the subsidiary ledgers to properly classify the amounts recorded; and

Prepare the Aging of Payables to keep track of any outstanding accounts to suppliers and facilitate their payments.
observations.managementAction:
The management communicated in writing to identified claimants and creditors their claims against LGU Santo Tomas. Once the balances are confirmed and verified, the LGU will release and remunerate the claims. In the case where the claimants or creditors waive their rights for the said claims, with a signed waiver, the management will adjust in favor of the 
LGU. It will be reverted to unappropriated surplus of the general fund which can be used to fund future programs, projects and activities of the LGU. The Accounting Office will provide the schedules/list of payables to the Treasurer’s Office to facilitate the communication since the latter maintains records of contact persons and/or numbers of the businesses that the LGU transacts with.
observations.reason:
The management was able to send confirmation letters to identified claimants or creditors for CY 2023.


Not Implemented
Unidentified Other Payables remain outstanding, as disclosed in the Notes to the Financial Statement.

Implemented
The Aging of Payables for CY 2023 was submitted by Management, and was duly validated by the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to require the following:

A written explanation from the collecting officers for the noted delays in the remittance and the retention of collections within an unreasonable period; and

The Revenue Collecting Officer is to remit their collections and accomplish the RCD at the close of each business day pursuant to Section 29 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Vol. 1 and see to it that no accumulation of cash from collections and mingling of funds shall be made by any collector to avoid loss, misapplication and/or unauthorized use.
observations.managementAction: The management demands the collecting officers to submit a written explanation for the noted delays in the remittance and the retention of their collection.
observations.reason:
The management has provided a written explanation to the Audit Team. 

Implemented
Analysis of the remittance of collections from August to December 2024 revealed no delays, and the RCD was accomplished at the close of each business day.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to undertake the following courses of action:

To ensure that all collections are deposited intact and daily, or on the next banking day pursuant to the provisions of Section 32 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; 

	To instruct the Municipal Treasurer to check the actual amounts of collections remitted by the collecting officer and to regularly update the cashbooks on the amount of collections and deposits made for the day; and 

Shall there be any cash overage incurred, demand a written explanation from the Accountable Officer concerned. If no satisfactory explanation was provided, issue OR for the amount of overage in favor of the government. In case of cash shortage, demand immediately the payment of the amount of shortage.
observations.managementAction: The management reminded and required the collecting officers to remit their collection, accomplish the report of collections and deposits at the close of each business day, and deposit all their collections intact to the authorized depository bank of Santo Tomas (Landbank Carmen Branch).
observations.reason:
The Municipal Treasurer ensured intact collections were deposited daily or on the next banking day.

Implemented

Implemented
Management ensured to issue an official receipt for any overage that may occur, and in case of a cash shortage, the payment shall be demanded immediately from the accountable officer.

observations.recommendation: See detailed recommendations embedded in the observation.
observations.managementAction:
In the selected samples of DVs form October to December 2023, the LGU comply the posting requirements in PhilGEPS.
 













The management will also ask the BAC to consolidate all office supplies requisition for the month and issue individual resolution recommending the mode of procurement to be used, especially if the said supplies were not available on the PS-DBM.  








The management will also remind all the departments/end users to avoid reimbursement as a mode of procurement. It will only be used in emergency cases or for reimbursement of expenses related to travelling and training. Part of the actions done by the management is to minimize the reimbursement transactions by setting threshold on the amount of reimbursement of an employee.
