We recommended that the
Municipal Mayor direct:
e. the Municipal Accountant to
request from the Provincial
Accountant the documents
needed as a basis for
recording the sanitary
landfill in their books; and
f. the Municipal Accountant
to obtain the documents from
the Provincial Accountant for
recording in the book of
accounts of the LGU.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant already obtained the documents from the Provincial Accountant for recording in the book of accounts of the LGU.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that
the Municipal Mayor instruct
the GAD Focal Point System to
strengthen the data gathering
and collating mechanisms of
the Municipality’s CBMS and
GAD databases through
preparation of master plan
indicating the timelines,
process, budget and other
necessary actions.
Thereafter, start the
implementation in CY 2021 in
order to ensure updated,
operational and reliable
information provided by said
CMBS and GAD databases which will serve in
attaining evidenced-based
planning, policy-making, and
identification of beneficiaries
for relief distribution and
financial assistance in the
future.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipality procured consulting services for the CBMS.
The data gathered are now being used by the Municipality. The implementation of said CMBS and GAD databases have already started.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that
the Local Municipality Mayor:
b. continuously tap the Barangay Officials to report to Building Official monthly/quarterly the on-going construction and completed structures in their barangays in order to facilitate the Building Official in monitoring compliance of the structures with the National Building Code of the Philippines and maximize income generated therewith;
d. direct the Municipal Assessor to verify whether real properties within the Municipality are properly listed in the assessment rolls, specifically those newly constructed or renovated.
observations.managementAction:
The Barangay Officials were directed by the LCE to comply with the AOM of the COA. As a result, all newly built and renovated structures obtained a Building Permit, thus increasing the income of the LGU.
The Municipal Assessment Office conducted a regular ocular inspection to determine if the newly constructed or renovated structures were listed in the assessment rolls.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that Management:

Direct the Municipal Accountant to effect immediately the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts amounting to ₱16,578.25 for a fair presentation of the balance of the cash in the bank and the liability accounts in the financial statements. 

Direct the Municipal Accountant to scrutinize the list of outstanding checks in the preparation of the monthly bank reconciliation statements to determine immediately    all checks that become stale to ensure a timely adjustment in the books of accounts.

Instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to be guided by Section 59, Volume 1 of the Manual on New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs) relative to the treatment of spoiled and stale checks.
observations.managementAction:
The management notified the payees/ suppliers/creditors of their outstanding cheques to know if they were still interested to claim the amounts payable to them. If they waive their rights on the said claims, adjustments will be taken in the books of accounts in favor of the LGU.

The Accounting Office copy furnished the Treasurer’s Office of the Monthly Bank Reconciliation in order to notify the payees before the checks become stale.

Conduct of physical inventory of the Land Improvement projects implemented by the LGU is scheduled on July 08, 2024 to August 02, 2024 based on the Physical Inventory Plan (PIP).

The management will conduct the necessary physical inventory of all the PPEs recorded under the books of accounts of the LGU. The management devised and designed a Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) that will not affect the original duties and functions performed by the designated/concerned officers in conducting the physical inventory.

It Is an on-going activity, scheduled from  July 2024 to March 2025.

Conduct of physical inventory of the Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE) owned by the LGU is scheduled on July 08, 2024 to December 27, 2024 based on the Physical Inventory Plan (PIP). The management will conduct the necessary physical inventory of all the PPEs recorded under the books of accounts of the LGU.
Once the Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) was implemented, the necessary data to accomplish the prescribed format of report will be filled up, enabling the accountable officers to comply with the COA audit recommendations.

It Is an on-going activity, scheduled from July 8, 2024 to December 27, 2024.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that management undertake the following courses of action:

Require the department heads who are part of the Inventory Committee to designate capable alternates to conduct the physical inventory of road networks.

Require the Municipal Engineer to spearhead the physical inventory of the road networks because of his technical knowledge and skills in engineering.

Require the Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects;

Require the General Services Officer or its equivalent to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components using the format prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015; and

Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network using the prescribed format as mentioned in the above circular and disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the total road network system.
observations.managementAction:
The management will conduct the necessary physical inventory of all the road networks account which will be spearheaded by Engineer Eugene Garcia. The committee will start with all the Road Projects that Engineer Garcia handled, monitored and documented from the time he was appointed as the municipal engineer of the Local Government Unit of Santo Tomas. In addition, the committee will inquire to the previous municipal engineer and engineering staffs for all the Road projects completed before 2016.
The Engineering Office was also preparing the Road Map to easily identify and locate each road network constructed from the year 2016 up to the present.
observations.reason: One of the identified problems is the tracing of records for Road Networks constructed before 2016. Because some of the road projects recorded in the books of account of the LGU were the result of compliance to COA Circular 2015-008 and adoption to PPSAS were previously closed infrastructure assets, including road networks to government equity, were recognized in the books of account.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that Management:

Direct the Municipal Treasurer (Acting GSO) to prepare a duly accomplished Inventory and Inspection Report for Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) to report all identified unserviceable properties of the LGU.

Direct the Disposal/Appraisal Committee headed by the Municipal Treasurer to conduct and manage the disposal process which includes the preparation of appraisal reports and other required documents to facilitate the disposal of the unserviceable properties following the procedures set forth under Section 379 of RA No. 7160, Section 167 of COA Circular No. 92-386 and Section 79 of PD No. 1445.

Avail the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, to establish property, plant, and equipment balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition, and accountability.
observations.managementAction:
Conduct physical inventory of the Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE) owned by the LGU is scheduled on July 08, 2024 to December 27, 2024 based on the Physical Inventory Plan (PIP). After the implementation of Property Inventory Plan (PIP), the management are expected to prepare the Inventory             and Inspection Report for Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) to report all identified unserviceable properties of the LGU. The management will conduct the necessary physical inventory of all the PPEs recorded under the books of accounts of the LGU.

The management intends to take advantage of the application of guidelines for one-time cleansing of PPE accounts which are also indicated in the Physical Inventory Plan devised by the management.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that Management:

Verify the validity of payables aged two years and more, send confirmation letters for balances with identified claimants or creditors, and remunerate all valid claims. Otherwise, cause the reversion of the payables to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund; 

Exert effort to retrieve the documents of unidentified Other Payables or those without details in the subsidiary ledgers to properly classify the amounts recorded; and

Prepare the Aging of Payables to keep track of any outstanding accounts to suppliers and facilitate their payments.
observations.managementAction:
The management was waiting on the accomplished reply form from the creditors to reconcile their claims with the recorded financial liabilities of the LGU. 

The management communicated in writing to identified claimants and creditors their claims against LGU Santo Tomas. Once the balances are confirmed and verified, the LGU will release and remunerate the claims. In the case where the claimants or creditors waive their rights for the said claims, with a signed waiver, the management will adjust the * favor of the LGU. It will be reverted to unappropriated surplus of the general fund which can be used to fund future programs, projects and activities of the LGU.
The Accounting Office will provide the schedules/list of payables to the Treasurer’s Office to facilitate the communication since the latter maintains records of contact persons and/or numbers of the businesses that the LGU transacts with.
observations.reason: The management was waiting on the accomplished reply form from the creditors to reconcile their claims with the recorded financial liabilities of the LGU.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to require the following:

A written explanation from the collecting officers for the noted delays in the remittance and the retention of collections within an unreasonable period; and

The Revenue Collecting Officer is to remit their collections and accomplish the Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD) at the close of each business day pursuant to Section 29 of the Manual on New Government Accounting System for LGUs, Vol. 1 and see to it that no accumulation of cash from collections and mingling of funds shall be made by any collector to avoid loss, misapplication and/or unauthorized use.
observations.managementAction: The management demands the collecting officers to submit a written explanation for the noted delays in the remittance and the retention of their collection.
observations.reason: The problem can be directly traced to the limited number of manpower in the Treasury Office, where there are only two (2) bonded employees who are allowed to prepare the necessary reports of collection.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to undertake the following courses of action:

To ensure that all collections are deposited intact and daily, or on the next banking day pursuant to the provisions of Section 32 of the Manual on New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUS), Volume I;

To instruct the Municipal Treasurer to check the actual amounts of collections remitted by the collecting officer and to regularly update the cashbooks on the amount of collections and deposits made for the day; and

Shall there be any cash overage incurred, demand a written explanation from the Accountable Officer concerned. If no satisfactory explanation was provided, issue OR for the amount of overage in favor of the government. In case of cash shortage, demand immediately the payment of the amount of shortage.
observations.managementAction: The management reminded and required the collecting officers to remit their collection, accomplish the report of collections and deposits at the close of each business day, and deposit all their collections intact to the authorized depository bank of Santo Tomas (Landbank Carmen Branch).
observations.reason: The problem can be directly traced to the limited number of service vehicle owned by the Municipality of Santo Tomas. These limited number of service vehicles were used by different offices to perform their duties and functions, but due to its scarcity, it cannot accommodate all the needs of the LGU for transportation. Though there are arrangement and scheduling done for the used of service vehicles owned by the LGU, sometimes there are circumstances where emergencies arises and there are inevitable swapping in schedules to be done to prioritize the other offices.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management require the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to ensure that vital information required to substantiate the validity and propriety of the claims is complete and properly accomplished before affixing their signatures on the disbursement vouchers and before effecting payment in compliance with Section 4 (6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445 and COA Circular 2012-001.
observations.managementAction: The management will inform the different departments of the LGU to properly accomplish the required data and information in the attachments of the disbursement voucher. The Local Finance Committee will notify them that their payment transactions will not be processed if the documentary requirements are incomplete and not properly accomplished.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated the following recommendations that Management requires:

The BAC to post the procurement opportunity on the PhilGEPS website for at least three (3) calendar days pursuant to Sections 54.2 and 54.3 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

The BAC to submit a BAC Resolution stating the mode of procurement adopted by the Management.

The Municipal Accountant to ensure that payments of goods or services procured by management are duly supported with BAC Resolution and printout copy of the posting on the PhilGEPS website.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the General Service Officer or its equivalent to require each department to prepare the APP-CSE for each year to be consolidated into one APP-CSE where the ordinary or regular supplies shall be procured on a quarterly basis at the DBM-PS as the first consideration. When unavailable, the items shall be procured through public bidding when the amount exceeds the threshold for shopping or small-value procurement.  Ensure that the conditions set forth by the law are strictly observed before resorting to alternative modes of procurement.

To minimize the recurrence of reimbursement as a practice of procuring common-use supplies, we have recommended that Management:

Ensure that all procurements of the LGU are meticulously and judiciously planned and within the approved budget of the Procuring Entity.

Procure ordinary and regular office supplies on a quarterly basis.

Require check payments to suppliers except for expenses that are petty in nature, non-recurring, and urgently needed, which shall be charged from the Petty Cash Fund.
observations.managementAction:
In the selected samples of disbursement vouchers form October to December 2023, the LGU comply the posting requirements in PhilGEPS. The management will also ask the Bids and Awards Committee to consolidate monthly requisitions of office supplies and issue individual resolutions recommending the mode of procurement to be used, especially if the said supplies were not available on the PS-DBM. 

The management will also remind all the departments/end users to avoid reimbursement as a mode of procurement. It will only be used in emergency cases or for reimbursement of expenses related to travelling and training. Part of the actions done by the management is to minimize the reimbursement transactions by setting threshold on the amount of reimbursement of an employee.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management require the Bids and Awards Committee to take immediate action to address the noted deficiencies and ensure that all lacking documents identified in the auditorial review, as enumerated in Annex A, are submitted to enable the Audit Team to establish the validity, legality and propriety of the procurement process on the implementation of the foregoing projects.
observations.managementAction:
The BAC Chairman and BAC Secretariat will submit the lacking documents enumerated in the Annex A of Audit Observation Memorandum 2024-019 to establish the validity and propriety of the transaction.
The management will also require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to ensure that the required supporting documents are complete before affixing signature in the disbursement vouchers and effecting payments.

observations.recommendation: Considering the limited manpower of the LGU, especially in the Accounting, Treasury, and Assessor’s Office, we have recommended the consideration and adoption of electronic or computerized systems for accounting, assessment, and collection processes. This would lead to timely and cost-efficient transaction processing and facilitate the preparation of reports, enabling management to comply and submit on time. Given the benefits it would provide to management and employees, we highly encourage the implementation of this recommendation.
observations.managementAction: The management, as part of the LGU commitment to comply with the digitization and digitalization of LGU processes, appropriated in the previous years a reasonable amount of Capital Outlay for Treasurer’s Office and Assessors Office for the planned procurement of additional IT equipment that will complement, suit and work well with system installed by the province. Recently, the province installed the Pangasinan Real Property Tax System (PARTS) to our Municipal Assessor’s Office in order to digitize our real property assessment process to be connected in the system used by our Treasurer’s Office. At present, the LGU officers and employees, both in Assessor and Treasurers Office, are familiarizing the system and will continue to undergo capability trainings to effectively used the PARTS.
observations.reason: LGU’s limited budget hinders the management to procure the necessary systems for processing of transactions and preparation of reports in accounting, assessment and collection.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer to adopt the ATM or digitized payroll system in the disbursement of payroll for salaries, wages and other emoluments of elected officials, regular and casual employees of the LGU. The adoption of a digitized payroll system is highly encouraged because it strengthens internal control on cash with improved delivery service to employees at reduced costs.
observations.managementAction:
The management communicated with the Land Bank-Carmen Rosales, as the agency’s authorized government servicing bank, the need of the LGU for ATM payroll system.

As of this writing, the LGU and its employees were scheduled to open their Payroll account in the Land Bank-Carmen Rosales.
observations.reason: The Land Bank will schedule the LGU employees for the necessary training that will equip the concerned offices of the vital skills in ATM payroll system adoption.

observations.recommendation: We recommend the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) in coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer to re-appropriate the unexpended balance or savings from the 20% Development Fund pertaining to previous years to finance the implementation of eligible development projects, thus the utilization of the said funds be optimized for the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environment management outcome of the LGU.
observations.managementAction:
As of this writing, the management submitted to the COA a copy of the appropriation ordinance to re-appropriate the unexpended balance or savings from the 20% Development Fund.

The Local Development Council recommended to reappropriate the savings or unexpended balance for the procurement of dump/garbage truck for the collection of solid waste of the entire municipality.

Management immediately addressed the audit issues by complying with the audit recommendations and providing the necessary justifications.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend the Municipal Mayor to direct the GAD Focal Person to:

carefully evaluate the suggested and/or attributed PPAs to ensure that these effectively address gender-related issues and concerns; and

shall regularly monitor if the GAD budget is being spent for what it was intended for and check on the actual cost of implementing the GAD Activities.
observations.managementAction:
In the HGDC the calculated score is 4-7.9 and it falls under promising GAD prospects (conditional pass) and enters in Gender- Sensitivity where there are 431 female farmers of about 1,026 males with a percentage of 42 % based on the RSBSA data. This farm to market road program and project is attributed to GAD Program. 

In addition to the Farm to Market Road CCTVs and Riprap are components of the opening and maintenance of Farm-to-market road Program, where the noted variance in GAD actual expenditure (excess over budget) includes the purchase of CCTVs.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior years’ recommendations and made additional recommendations and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:

require the Acting-Municipal Accountant to (i) ensure that all purchases of supplies and materials are recorded under the proper inventory account; and (ii) prepare supplies ledger cards for delivered items as called for in the above-quoted regulation;

inform the BAC, Municipal Health Officer and Acting-Municipal Accountant to (i) submit documentations required cited under Annex A of this Memorandum, for further evaluation by the Audit Team; and (ii) examine noted deviations in Item 1.19 and 1.29, for future similar transactions, adhere closely with the requirements of the law preceding to payment thereof; and

instruct the GSO to (i) conduct periodic inventory of supplies as prescribed by the above regulations; and (ii) make an effort to procure directly from the PS-DBM all anticipated common-use supplies and resort to local suppliers only upon unavailability of supplies needed.

We restated and made additional recommendations and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the BAC and BAC Secretariat, in coordination with other concerned departments to:

improve the quality and extent of their compliance with the requirements of RA 9184, its IRR, and relevant rules and regulations in the Municipality’s procurement process by: (i) paying attention to the procurement lapses noted above, to avoid the recurrence thereof in future procurements; (ii) preparation of Project Procurement Management Plan (PPMP) as basis for the preparation of the Annual Procurement Plan (APP); (iii) on future similar undertakings on infrastructure projects, conduct of complete detailed engineering prior to the preparation of bid documents in order to assure the viability of the infrastructure project; (iv) attending the mandatory trainings required for personnel involved in procurement and regularly checking for updates on procurement and if possible, attending webinars on the latest issuances pertaining to procurement and other relevant rules and regulations at least once a year, or as needed, as prescribed under the revised implementing rules and regulations of RA 9184 to keep abreast with the latest issuances and be able to comply with the prescribed requirements accordingly; and (v) submission of the lacking documents appearing in Annex A, for further evaluation as the basis for the Audit Team’s subsequent audit action.

necessitate the Acting Municipal Accountant to check the completeness of the required supporting documents for every transaction before any payment is made; and

consider hiring a permanent Municipal Planning and Development Officer and additional staff to assist in the conduct of functions of the BAC Secretariat.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendations and made additions thereto that the Local Chief Executive direct the LDRRMO to:

 together with the LDRRMC, prepare and submit annually the LDRRMFIP in the prescribed format with details such as allotment class in reference to COA Circular 2012-002, inclusive of the plan relative to the unexpended balance of the fund for the current year and the trust fund for DRRM; and 

regularly monitor implementation thereof and maximize the fund to avoid non-attainment of the target objectives of the fund; and

include in its plan the gradual compliance with the said standards on disaster preparedness based on available resources.

We reiterated and supplemented our previous year’s recommendations and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the:

GAD Focal Point Person (GFPP) to (i) in coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development Officer (MPDO), work on the complete establishment of a GAD database and the maintenance thereof for a more gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation;  (ii) make a representation with the Budget Office during the budget deliberation seeing to it that at least the required 5% of the total appropriations for the year is budgeted or attributed for the ensuing year; (iii) amend the GPB to match the pre-prepared GPB with the approved appropriation in the annual budget for the budget year, subject to the review and approval of the DILG; (iv) closely monitor the implementation of the programs and projects embodied in the approved GPB by the DILG to ensure full attainment of the GAD targets/objectives, intervene immediately if delays in the implementation are observed and amend the GPB, if warranted; and

Municipal Accountant to adhere with the requirements on the preparation of Subsidiary Ledgers in consonance with COA Circular 2021-008 dated September 6, 2021.

We reiterated and supplemented our prior years’ audit recommendations that the Local Chief Executive: 

in close coordination with the Treasurers’ Office and Accounting Department, (i) make sure that the same amount withheld is remitted the following month; and (ii) look into the breakdown of the variances from the withheld amount and remittances starting off with the current year and subsequently the previous years and make necessary adjustments, if warranted; 

as part of the internal control system, designate/appoint a person who will check all deductions and remittances made to make sure all are accounted for; and 

provide the Accounting Office with additional personnel, if possible, to aid in the preparation of the Index of Payment and immediate detection of errors in remittances.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct:
a. The Inventory Committee:
• immediately after the conduct of complete physical count, to reconcile
inventory results with property and accounting records; and
• to ensure that all counted items are properly specified and identified
detailing physical attributes and location.
b. The Property and Accounting units to perform reconciliation of the PPE balance
particularly on the noted difference of ₱8,886,878.88 and make the necessary
adjustments/recording of entries, if any;
c. The City Accountant to disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements the
results of reconciliation of RPCPPE and accounting records as well as the
amount of PPE items, if any, without available supporting documents; and
d. The existing Inventory Committee or a newly created Inventory Committee to
study the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 on one-time cleansing of PPE and
consider its implementation to account for the non-existing PPEs and those
PPEs that lost criteria to be recognized as PPE and to present reliable balances
in the financial statements.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Treasurer to:
a. prepare the Certified List of Taxpayers based on the Real Property Tax Account
Register/Taxpayer’s index card, showing the name of taxpayers and the amount
due and collectible for the year; and
b. furnish the City Accountant the duly certified list of taxpayers, to be the basis
of the accounting for establishing/setting-up of the correct receivables at the
beginning of the year in compliance with Section 20 of the MNGAS for LGUs,
Volume 1.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to:
a. recognize income in the Trust Fund books upon utilization of funds received
from national agencies or any other sources, whether private or government,
that are considered as non-exchange transactions and record all entries
relevant to the transactions; and
b. prepare and submit in the succeeding years, the Statement of Financial
Performance for the Trust Fund along with the other financial statements of the
City.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to ensure that
DRRM funds received from other sources will be treated as special trust liability
recorded under Trust Liabilities – DRRMF in the Trust Fund books instead of Other
Payables account.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:
a. City Health Officer to:
- obtain from DOH Central Office, the actual information on the unit cost and
quantities of the COVID-19 vaccines by the City; and
- render a monthly report to the City Accounting Office on the receipt,
issuance and balances of vaccines directly received from outside source.
b. City Accountant:
- to coordinate with the CHO to determine and recognize in the books the full
cost of the ending inventory of vaccines as at year-end and to record future
receipts and issuances of the same.
Henceforth, comply with the provisions of Paragraph 9 of IPSAS 12 and COA
Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 01, 2015 regarding the proper recording of
receipt and utilization of allocated COVID-19 vaccines from the DOH and other
agencies.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the concerned disbursing officers to
provide justifications for the late liquidation of their cash advances.

We recommended that:
a. The members of the SP update the CLUP ahead of the CDP to guide in the
selection, prioritization and screening of projects, programs and activities in the
CDP; and
b. The members of the LDC chaired by the LCE facilitate the preparation and
update of the CDP of the City consistent with the guidelines in the preparation
of the CDP to prevent the wastage of funds and ensure that PPAs implemented
under the 20% Development are truly the inputs from the CDP towards the
realization of the City’s development targets.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the CPDO and the City Engineer to
prioritize the inclusion in the 20% DF of projects that are allowed to be charged to
the fund pursuant to DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 and those projects which are
incidental to effective and efficient local governance and for the promotion of
general welfare of every constituent in the City as per DBM-DOF-DILG Joint
Memorandum Circular No. 1.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the CDRRM Officer to:
a. Submit within 60 days from receipt thereof a duly revised LDRRMFIP which
includes unexpended LDDRMF in prior years;
b. Promptly comply the required annual preparation and updating of the
LDRRMFIP with defined projects or activities, and strictly comply with the
requirement before the release and use of the unexpended previous year’s
DRRM balance accruing to the special trust fund; and
c. Provide the Audit Team proof of submission of the Annual LDRRMFIP to the
OCD and DILG-LGOO within fifteen calendar days from receipt hereof in
accordance with the provision of Section 5.1.6 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the CDRRM Officer to:
a. Submit within 60 days from receipt thereof the City’s plan for the implementation
of unutilized balance of LDRRM Fund amounting to P36,095,484.13;
b. Submit within 15 days from receipt thereof a written justifications and
explanations regarding the above observation on unutilized LDRRM Fund, the
ineligible charges to the disaster fund and those expenses not present in
LDRRMFIP; and
c. Judiciously plan on how to maximize the utilization of the LDRRM Fund based
on the approved LDRRMFIP and to accelerate their implementation in order to
achieve the goal/target of the City regarding disaster relief, recovery, and risk
reduction and management.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
a. City engineer to ensure that repair, rectification and correction of the noted
deficiencies will be accomplished and completed by the contractor and submit
to the Audit Team the status of compliance for submission to the COA TAS,
otherwise, refund the noted deficiencies and defects totaling P603,155.20 plus
liquidated damages, if warranted; and
b. City Accountant to deduct from the claims of the concerned contractors the
deficiencies noted if no action was undertaken pursuant to 2016 Revised IRR of
RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the City observe guidelines provided in Section C.1 (5) of
Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of the Philippine Commission on Women,
Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG), the Department of Budget
and Management, and the National Economic and Development Authority, to
effectively plan and implement GAD PPAs of the LGU.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the CGSO to:
a. retrieve the records of the properties with no tags and facilitate the
placement/attachment of such Property Tags/Stickers on all properties for easy
cross-referencing with the AREs and proper identification of the properties; and
b. attach property tags to the newly acquired PPEs before distribution to the
intended end-users to properly monitor and establish control on the use of the
government property and equipment.

We recommended that the|LCE creates an         |Inventory Committee to |conduct an inventory of|road networks in        |compliance with the     |requirement of COA      |Circular No. 2015-008   |and Submit the          |corresponding reports   |to the Audit Team for   |evaluation and          |verification.
observations.managementAction: The management|will constitute an Inventory|Committee to comply all|the requirements provided|in COA Circular No.     |2015-008.
observations.reason: To conduct an inventory|and assessment of all local|road networks within the|municipality to ensure|the reconciliation with|accounting records and|to submit the reports|to the Commission on|Audit for verification.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the|LCE enjoins the        |concerned officials to |ensure the annual       |preparation of the      |required reports,       |conduct of inventory of |roads and submission of |the corresponding report|on the physical inventory|taken to the Office of  |the Auditor within the  |prescribed period.
observations.managementAction: To conduct an     |inventory and assessment|of all local road      |networks within the     |municipality to ensure  |the reconciliation with |accounting records and   |to submit the reports   |to the Commission on    |Audit for verification.
observations.reason: There is a physical movement restriction among the team because of the onset COVID-19 pandemic.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the BAC Chairman impose retention money for projects whose Statement of Work Accomplished is 50% and below, in conformity with Item 6.1, Annex “E” of the RA No. 9184 to safeguard the Municipality for uncorrected discovered defects and third-party liabilities by the contractors.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality will withhold the retention money for the next billing.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipality revert/refund to the 20% Development Fund the amount of P233,415.04 pertaining to the penalties incurred due to late payment of loan amortizations and will be subject for re-appropriation to finance development projects.

Likewise, we recommended that the concerned officials and employees consult the Municipal Development Office of the Department of Finance on possible remedies for the waiver or reconsideration on the payment of penalties made, if any, otherwise, the concerned officials and employees should refund to the Municipality’s account the said penalties.
observations.managementAction:
The Management was not able to revert the P233,415.04 to Development Fund.



The Management had coordinated with the MDFO, however, no response yet for the reconsideration.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor grant the payment of honoraria sourced from savings of the projects, as authorized by the Sanggunian, if the collection from sale of bid documents is insufficient.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor had directed that the payment of honoraria be sourced from the projects’ savings.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the local government unit to instruct the General Services Officer/Municipal Treasurer to prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report for Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) as a basis to record the dropping from the books of the unserviceable properties and undertake the necessary actions in accordance with the provisions of Section 379 of RA 7160.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that the Municipal Treasurer will prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report for Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) to comply with the provisions of Section 379 of Republic Act 7160.
observations.reason: As of December 31, 2021, the management was not able to prepare and submit the IIRUP, thus the disposal of unserviceable properties were still not implemented.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that management: 

Strictly adhere to the provisions of the procurement laws, rules and regulations and ensure that all procurement were sourced from suppliers that are duly registered with the Philippine Government Electronic Procurement System (PhilGEPS). 


To observe the submission of the PhilGEPS Registration Number issued to suppliers as an eligibility requirement in compliance with Section 54.6 of Republic Act No. 9184 in order to promote transparency in the procurement activities of the municipality under the alternative methods of procurement.
observations.managementAction: The management enforced the registration requirement of suppliers with the Philippine Government Electronic Procurement System (PhilGEPS) for its procurement done through the alternative methods of procurement and attach quotations from three different suppliers.
observations.reason:
Not Implemented

Verification of the suppliers of the LGU revealed that only CICXpress Printing Shop and Onetsky Catering were duly registered with PhilGEPs on CY 2021.



Not Implemented

The other suppliers of the LGU were still not PhilGEPs registered.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended to the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) the following:

Deficiencies noted be given immediate action and relevant documents needed be timely submitted;


Strictly adhere to the pertinent provisions of RA No. 9184 specifically Section 23 and 25.2 (a) and (b) and 

Comply with the documentary requirements as specified in COA Circular No. 2012-001 to enable the Audit Team to evaluate projects completely, establish the validity, legitimacy and propriety of the transaction.
observations.managementAction: The Bids and Awards Committee, headed by Municipal Engineer, will ensure the submission of the relevant documents and comply with the audit recommendations.
observations.reason:
Required documents of some projects were still not submitted.


Not Implemented

Management was not able to comply with the recommendation.


Not Implemented

Management was not able to comply with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended Management the following:

To require concerned officials and employees to submit the corresponding Official Receipts/ Sales/Cash Invoice of the transactions mentioned above.



For the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to ensure that supporting documents are completely and properly accomplished before affixing their signatures in the disbursement vouchers and before effecting payments pursuant to Section 4 (6) of Presidential Decree No.1445 to avoid issuance of Notice of Suspension.

Provisions of the laws, rules and regulations pertaining to documentation of claims against government funds should be strictly complied with.
observations.managementAction: The management required the concerned officials and employees to submit the necessary documents to support the said disbursement. The management assured that they will look or ask for the duplicate copy of the Official Receipts/Sales/ Cash Invoice, certified by the business owner or its authorized representative, for the mentioned transactions.
observations.reason:
The Official receipts were still not submitted to the Office of the Auditor.




Fully Implemented

Based on the post audit for CY 2021 transactions, the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer ensured that the pertinent official receipts were attached to the disbursement vouchers.


Fully Implemented

Management complied with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipality ensure that payment of goods and services are made directly to the suppliers or dealers through the issuance of checks except in cases where needed items are highly necessary and urgent or petty in nature and requires to be paid immediately where payments through checks would be deemed impractical in compliance with Section 2 of COA Circular 97-002.
observations.managementAction: The management discussed with the concerned officials and employees that payment through reimbursement basis will be applicable only in the payment of travelling expenses and highly necessary and urgent or petty in nature and requires to be paid immediately wherein payments through checks would be deemed impractical.
observations.reason: As per post audit of CY 2021 transactions, several reimbursements of supplies and materials that are not urgent and petty in nature were still paid by the LGU.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management require the Municipal Accountant to ensure completeness of supporting documents before affixing his signature in the disbursement vouchers and before effecting payments pursuant to Section 4 (6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445 to avoid suspension in audit. Compliance with the provisions of the law, rules and regulations pertaining to documentation of claims against government funds is strictly recommended.
observations.managementAction: The management, through the Office of the Municipal Accountant, will submit the required documentary requirements in accordance with Section 4 of Presidential Decree 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001
observations.reason: Some of the required documents mentioned were still not submitted.

observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2020 | The Management created Inventory Team in which the personnel came from different concerned departments to be assigned in the inventory of the supplies, equipment and other properties of the Municipality.  The Management also created/hired new personnel (Architect I) in the Office of the Municipal Engineer to monitor, conduct and facilitate the inventory of the Local Road Networks. They are also instructed to conduct a physical count of the properties of the Municipality.

AAR CY 2020 | The management created/hired additional personnel in the Office of the Municipal Accountant to take charge and assist in the preparation of the documents needed and proper recording of all the supplies purchased/procured. A storage/stock room is included in the ongoing Municipal Building Extension

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer:

continue to exert extra efforts to trace prior years’ records so that remittances maybe made possible and see to it that all taxes withheld during the current year are remitted intact and immediately to the BIR within the prescribed period as required by Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 23-2007 dated March 23, 2007 and coordinate with the BIR for the proper disposition of prior years’ account;

properly maintain subsidiary ledgers and to make proper transfer of records in case of employees’ turnover to facilitate the immediate preparation of the reports pertaining to disbursements payable to the BIR; and

adhere strictly to the prescribed period in the remittance of taxes to the BIR.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY- 2020 | The two concerned departments will exert efforts to trace and reconcile the agency’s Due to BIR Balance and to continue the practice of withholding accurate taxes and remitting the same at the prescribed time.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive 

(a) instruct end users in the next budgeting period to prepare Project Procurement Management Plan (PPMP) as basis of the budget for the ensuing year; 

(b) require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Secretariat to consolidate the latter based on submitted PPMPs, as basis in the preparation of the Annual Procurement Plan (APP); and 

(c) cause the Acting General Services Office (GSO) Head to 

(i) furnish COA copies of all purchase orders/contracts and all papers relative thereto within five (5) days after the perfection of the contract and 

(ii) send notification of the time and date of scheduled deliveries to the Office of the Auditor pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 and 95-006.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2020 | The Management instructed the concerned departments to comply to the recommendation of the Audit team as to preparation of PPMP as basis for the ensuing year and the basis for the formulation of APP. And to forward the needed documents to the Audit Team before the deliveries of purchases.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive 

(a) strictly enforce the Sangguniang Bayan Ordinance on the “No Segregation, No Collection” and “No Littering” policies so that penalties may be imposed on the violators of environmental laws; and 

(b) initiate intensive information drive and awareness campaign among the residents in coordination with barangay officials on the implementation of the five basic environmental laws on preserving and protecting the environment required under R.A. No. 9003.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2020 | The LGU strictly advised the Barangay to use their MRF for their waste/garbage disposal and those who are still depending in Central or LGU MRF, the management will strictly implement the SB Ordinance “No Segregation, No Collection Policy.” and to include in their Barangay Budget the construction of their own MRF.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the officials to prioritize the registration and transfer of titles of the 157 parcels of land, in compliance with Section 39 of PD No. 1445 and Section 148 of the Rules and Regulations on Supply and Property Management in the Local Governments.
observations.managementAction: No management action was made
observations.reason: Awaiting Sanggunian Resolution, approved plan with budget and available fund for land surveying

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: • reorganize the composition of City Solid Waste Management Board in accordance with Section 12 of RA No. 9003; and • instruct the City Solid Waste Management Board to assist all barangays in the reestablishment of an individual Materials Recovery Facility (MRF) and ensure the City’s MRF is existing and fully operational. Also update and review Ten-Year Solid Waste Management Plan.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that they are in process of updating Solid Waste Management Plan for 2022 and succeeding years. As of 2022, 22 barangays have already established MRF.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Assessor to undertake securing of titles from the Land Registration Authority (LRA) and proper documentation.
observations.managementAction: City Assessor has still not undertaken securing of titles from LRA due to lack of designated personnel to process applications.
observations.reason: Certificates of Title were not yet secured from the LRA due to lack of designated personnel to process the applications.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Chairman of the Committee on Disposal to initiate/facilitate the immediate disposal of the subject properties in accordance with cited provisions in order not to expose those properties to further deterioration or loss of salvaged value and to generate additional income thereof.
observations.managementAction: GSO submitted report of unserviceable properties but report returned for compliance for lack of supporting documents; as of writing, report not returned; Committee on Disposal had not initiated disposal.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Health Officer and the Municipal Budget Officer to spearhead the necessary adjustments so that the Community Isolation Unit of the Municipality may satisfy and meet the requirements set forth in the guidelines for the certification/accreditation; and
Municipal Health Officer to keep abreast of the new pronouncements or benefit packages that may aid the LGU in addressing the pandemic.
Thereafter, we also recommended that Municipal Mayor start an initiative for the accreditation of the Municipality’s Community Isolation Unit, whether by sending an application for accreditation if necessary, or by requesting the Department of Health to conduct another site visitation of the CIU.  Thereafter, the Municipality to request for accreditation from PhilHealth in order to avail of the CCBIP.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to: 1) Investigate the details of the Due to National Government Agencies account in the amount of P2,378,373.07; 2) monitor fund releases and implementation of the projects; and 3) maintain records relative thereto; and
Municipal Accountant to:  1) file necessary records on the releases and payments of funds receive from other government agencies; 2) refund the savings from LGSF AM to Bureau of Treasury; and 3) reconcile her records with that of MPDC’s periodically.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Human Resources Officer-Designate to fill-up the vacant positions in order that the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator may free himself from other duties and be able to concentrate on his responsibilities as MPDC, such as monitoring the funds to be released to the LGU and immediate compliance on requirements set forth by other agencies, among others.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor follow-up the release of their share on Excise Tax for FY 2017 collections under Republic Act Nos. 7171 and 8240.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
the end-user/implementing unit to closely monitor the implementation of projects so that the supposed benefits to be derived therefrom may be realized; and
the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to:
thoroughly plan and identify projects that are feasible and compliant with Local Budget Memorandum No. 76; and
recommend re-programming of projects that were not feasible to the Sangguniang Bayan.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GFPS to include additional programs which are organization-focused and optimally utilized the GAD Fund in order fully achieve women’s empowerment and gender equality.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive to designate a General Service Officer (GSO) who will handle the property management of the Municipality and instruct the GSO to prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report for Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) as a basis to record the dropping from the books of the unserviceable properties and undertake the necessary actions in the disposal of the unserviceable properties in accordance with Section 79 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 92-386.

We have also recommended that after the disposal the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to drop the unserviceable properties from the books of accounts in accordance with IPSAS 17 and other existing accounting and auditing rules and regulations.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive to require the General Service Officer or the Local Treasurer as the case maybe, to:
Facilitate and implement the attachment of property identification tags/sticker for each item of PPE of the agency for proper identification of properties; and
Prepare the report of physical count in the prescribed format in accordance to NGAS for LGUs and properly install a strong internal control in safeguarding the government properties.

We have recommended the following:
That the personnel in-charge of the fund to closely monitor the balance in order to generate timely reports that will serve as basis in order to transfer the unexpended/unobligated QRF/DRRM-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund under the account Trust Liabilities-DDRM; and
That the local government unit to transfer all unexpended/unobligated balance of the Quick Response Fund and the Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liabilities – Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund” (account number 2-04-01-020) in the Trust Fund book.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive to ensure that payment of goods and services are made directly to the suppliers or dealers through the issuance of checks except in cases where needed items are highly necessary and urgent or petty in nature and requires to be paid immediately or when payments through checks would be deemed impractical in compliance with Section 2 of COA Circular 97-002.

We have further recommended that management adopt public bidding or alternative modes of procurement provided under RA No. 9184 to promote transparency and to avoid possible source of irregularities.

We have recommended that the management require the concerned personnel to submit the approved Purchase Orders with its supporting documents pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009, in order to facilitate the review and evaluation process of the transactions.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive/Municipal Mayor to direct the assigned personnel to submit the copies of perfected contracts to the Audit Team within five (5) working days as prescribed in Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 in order to allow the Audit Team to perform effective review and generate timely and relevant audit results.

Strict compliance as provided in Section 4 of the same circular against the concerned employee to avoid imposition of administrative disciplinary action for unjustified failure to submit the documentary requirements within the timelines.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive to:
Instruct the BAC Chairman, BAC Secretary and Municipal Accountant to submit the required documents enumerated herein to establish the validity and propriety of the transaction. It is to be emphasized herein that non-compliance on the submission may result in the issuance of Notice of Suspension.
Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the required supporting documents are complete before affixing her signature on the voucher and effecting payment.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive to:
Instruct the Municipal Engineer to comply with the posting of Signboards in the location where the PPAs were carried out indicating therein the relevant information detailed in Item 2.1 of COA Circular 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013; and
Furnish the Office of the Auditor the necessary information within ten (10) days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity and post signboards and/or public notices in the project site in compliance with Section 3.1 of COA Circular No. 2013-04 and in order for us to validate the infrastructure projects on time.

We have reiterated our recommendation that management:
Strictly adhere to the provisions of the procurement laws, rules and regulations and ensure that all procurement were sourced from suppliers that are duly registered with the Philippine Government Electronic Procurement System (PhilGEPS).
To observe the submission of the PhilGEPS Registration Number issued to suppliers as an eligibility requirement in compliance with Section 54.6 of Republic Act No. 9184 in order to promote transparency in the procurement activities of the municipality under the alternative methods of procurement.

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and other municipal officials concerned to intensify their efforts to collect the delinquent real property tax. It is further recommended that management institute available remedies provided under the aforementioned Sections of the Local Government Code.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Treasurer to device measures to validate the declared gross receipts of establishments applying for permits to engage in business especially those who are having direct transactions with the LGU.

We reiterated that the Local Chief Executive:
stop the practice of charging fuel, oil and lubricants expenses of private vehicles against Municipal funds; and
concerned drivers to prepare the trip ticket judiciously prior to trips leading to the completion of data needed in the trip tickets.
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the:
GSO to prepare and submit Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption as called for by the above quoted regulation to properly monitor the fuel consumption of the LGU;
OIC-Municipal Accountant to (i) check the completeness of the information provided in the trip ticket prior to allowing payment thereof; and (ii) use correct/specific accounts in the Chart of Accounts as basis for reliable budgeting in subsequent years; and
Municipal Budget Officer to refrain from allowing charges not in consonance with the actual expenditures submitted, to be able to have an accurate data of distinct expenditures for reliable budgeting in future years to come.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive:
instruct the OIC-Acting Municipal Accountant to review the noted transactions and prepare the appropriate adjusting journal entries to correct the misstatements of the affected accounts. Henceforth, ensure that costs of major repairs, rehabilitation, improvement, and construction of infrastructure assets should be capitalized or recognized as property, plant and equipment in accordance with International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) No. 17 and related rules on asset recognition; and
direct the Municipal Budget Officer to adequately appropriate funds classified under Capital Outlays instead of allotments for MOOE in the LGU’s Annual Budget for transactions that are capital in nature such as construction and major repairs of buildings and infrastructures to facilitate the correct accounting recognition as required by Section 50 of Manual on NGAS for LGUs Volume I.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive submit/present a legal basis for the release of the 50% share of the PNP for CY 2021. Otherwise, discontinue the release of the fund.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendations that the Local Chief Executive (a) require the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, Municipal Budget Officer and the Local Development Council coordinate efforts to present the PPAs under the 20% DF in the AIP, in sufficient detail as required above; and (b) instruct the BAC Members, Municipal Engineer and MPDC to properly plan and strictly utilize the Fund according to the plan, to ensure that the Fund is utilized for its intended purpose.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive direct the LDRRMO to include in its plan, the gradual compliance with the said standards on disaster preparedness based on available resources.
Furthermore, we also recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
direct the LDRRMO together with the LDRRMC, to prepare and submit annually the LDRRMFIP for the DRRM program of the LGU, in compliance with the prescribed format with details such as allotment class, including list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years, among others, as prescribed by the above quoted regulations;
instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to prepare the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization within the prescribed period and submit the same to the LDRRMO and finally to the COA Auditor; and
instruct the General Services Officer to prepare quarterly reports on the receipt and distribution/utilization/issuance of in-kind donations and submit the same within the prescribed period together with the supporting documents as mentioned above.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation and the Management agreed that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, to submit financial reports and documents within the first ten (10) days of the ensuing month, and strictly adhere with the requirements on the timely submission of accounts and financial reports, pursuant to COA Circular Nos. 95-006 and 2009-006.
Further, we recommended that the LCE consider the augmentation of the Municipal Accountant’s workforce to improve its timeliness on the submission of the required reports and accounts.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendations that the Local Chief Executive:
exercise strong political will to implement the applicable Sangguniang Bayan Ordinance on the “No Segregation, No Collection” and “No Littering” policies so that penalties may be imposed on the violators of environmental laws; and
initiate intensive information drive and awareness campaign among the residents, in coordination with barangay, following the strategies identified under the approved 10-year solid waste management plan of the LGU in conjunction with the five basic environmental laws on preserving and protecting the environment required under R.A. No. 9003.
Further, we recommended that the LCE initiate the creation of MENR Office or appointment of a person other than the current designated MENRO, who will be responsible for the implementation of laws, rules and regulations pertinent to the environment and natural resources.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive:
in close coordination with the Treasurers’ Office and Accounting Department, (i) trace the unremitted balances starting from the current and recent periods to bring about the proper remittance of the amounts; and (ii) make sure that the same amount withheld is remitted the following month;
instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to maintain a complete Index of Payments for proper monitoring of claims and for prompt and accurate recording and remittances to GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG; and
consider the employment of a permanent Municipal Accountant to oversee all the financial transactions of the Municipality, including the withholding and remittance of GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:

a. City Accountant and City GSO to evaluate the causes of the material
discrepancy of P289,569,333.25, to ascertain the fairness and reliability of
the PPE balances in the financial statements; and

b. City GSO, together with the Inventory Committee, to adopt the guidelines
prescribed in COA Circular No. 2020-006 in the conduct of complete
physical count of the properties and reconciliation of the results thereof
with accounting records and afterwards submit the same to the Audit Team
to ascertain the existence, validity and correctness of the PPE, as well as
the reliability of balances presented in the financial statements.

B. Presence of beginning/unreconciled balances in the Schedules of PPE
prepared and submitted by the Accounting Office in the amount of P9.576
million.

Verification of the Schedules of PPE submitted by the Accounting Office revealed
that various PPE accounts totaling P9,576,368.22 were described as unreconciled
balances at the beginning of the year that due to insufficient or absence of records
can no longer be traced as to type or quantity. Interview with the City Accountant
disclosed that those beginning/unreconciled balances have no available records to
clearly identify the breakdown of such balances as to nature and kind. Those PPEs
have date of acquisition from prior years, which cannot be determined as to date of
origin up to 1999, while most have no dates indicted. Due to absence of records,
these PPEs remained in a lump sum amount and not separated per item of PPEs.

The list of those old balances are provided below, as follows:

Amounts
Account Title
GF SEF Total
Other Structures P 418,000.00 P 0.00 P 418,000.00
Other Machinery and Equipment 56,100.00 0.00 56,100.00
Furniture and Fixture 7,533,248.22 1,569,020.00 9,102,268.22
Total P8,007,348.22 P1,569,020.00 P9,576,368.22

Existence of unidentified and/or long outstanding PPE casted doubt on the validity,
reliability and verifiability of the accounts as recorded in the books of the City.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to make
detailed evaluation and tracing of the noted unreconciled beginning balances
to reflect the reliable balances of these accounts in the financial statements.

C. The City still failed to comply with the proper reporting and disclosure of Road
Networks in the City’s financial statements, as the recorded gross amount of
P214.884 million at the end of the year remained not supported with inventory
report, ledger cards, property cards, report on local road networks and
required disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements.

As in prior years’ audit findings, the concerned City officials still failed to submit to the
Audit Team the required inventory reports and failed to present individual Local Road
Networks Ledger Cards and Local Road Networks Property Cards for Road
Networks and its component worth P214,884,724.20 as of December 31, 2021.
Verification of the status of implementation and review of the submitted reports
showed the following:

a) The CY 2021 RPCPPE included the result of physical count for its Road
Networks account. However, the Report of Local Road Network was not provided
which should show Road ID No., Segregation per component and cost per road
component.

b) The inventory report, property cards ledger cards and the disclosure in the Notes
to Financial Statements were still not prepared/complied by the City GSO and
City Accounting Office, respectively, as of this date.

c) The City GSO still failed to prepare the Physical Count of Local Road Network
and submit it to the City Accounting Office for reconciliation. Instead, the City
GSO included the Road Networks in its RPCPPE. Further, the Road Networks in
the RPCPPE were not segregated per road component and also lacked
information on cost per road component, road network no. and condition per road
component.

d) It was still noted that the computation of depreciation expense for each project
recorded under the Road Networks were determined in lump sum amount and
not per component. Each of the road and its components have different
estimated useful life, thus segregation of cost of road components of local roads
for separate computation of depreciation expense is required. Likewise, road lot
components of the road networks are not to be subjected to depreciation. But
still, it was noted that the depreciation for CY 2021 was computed based on the
total cost of the road network without considering the road components, and
using a uniform estimated useful life of 120 months or 10 years. It was observed
that since road components of road networks were not segregated, the estimated
useful life per component cannot be established, thus uniform application of
depreciation was made by the Accounting Office in the lapsing schedule.
Likewise, non-segregation of road component had hindered the Accounting
Office to identify the road lot which, according to the Circular cannot be subject
for depreciation.

We reiterated that the City Mayor require the:

a. City Engineer to provide the City Accountant and City GSO with the
complete description and cost segregation of road components for road
projects;

b. City Accountant to prepare and maintain the prescribed property ledger
cards for Local Road Networks, disclose the information for the said
property in the Notes to Financial Statements in accordance with the above
cited regulations, and provide separate depreciation for the road and the
road components;

c. City GSO to periodically maintain a complete Report on Local Road
Networks, prepare the Road Map and keep a complete Local Road Network
Property Cards for all roads and their components, and furnish the
concerned offices of the copies thereof, particularly the City Accountant,
as basis for reconciliation with the accounting records; and

d. Inventory Committee, in coordination with the City Engineering Office, to
(i) conduct physical inventory of all existing local road networks in the City,
and prepare the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Local Road
Network to be reconciled with the accounting records; and (ii) furnish a
copy of such report to the Audit Team for verification and audit.

D. Non-compliance with the prescribed format, incomplete information and
erroneous preparation of the RPCPPE, contrary to Section 57, Chapter II of the
Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local
Government Units (LGUs), Volume II.

Section 57, Chapter II of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II provides that “The Report on
the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (Annex 49) shall be used to
report the physical count of property, plant and equipment by type as of a given date.
It shows the balance of property and equipment per cards and per count and
shortage/overage, if any.” The same shall also be submitted to the Auditor
concerned not later than January 31 of each year.

The Audit Team praises the efforts exerted by the City GSO and his staff in the
continuous conduct of physical count and the preparation and submission of the
RPCPPE. However, the report was again submitted late to the Audit Team on
February 16, 2022 rather than the January 31 deadline.

Further, the Audit Team noted that the report was still not compliant with the format
prescribed by Annex 49 of MNGAS for LGUs despite the recurring audit observations
provided in the Annual Audit Reports (AARs) for CYs 2019 and 2020.

Although the submitted RPCPPE by the GSO has Description, Unit and Total Costs,
Quantity and Property Code, it still lacked vital information on the following:

a) Name of Accountable Officer
b) Official Designation
c) Date of Assumption
d) Article or Property Classification
e) Property Number
f) Unit of Measure
g) Balance per Card
h) On Hand per Count
i) Shortage/Overage
j) Remarks

The report was still not completely filled up and lacked vital information pertaining the
actual result of the physical inventory count undertaken. These sets of information
were necessary to determine whether the actual assets counted agreed with the
recorded accountability. Likewise, such portions of the RPCPPE will technically entail
the “actual result” of the physical count undertaken (“On Hand per Count” column),
the determination of missing, lost, unlocated or excess properties, if there are any
(“Shortage/Overage” column) and the whereabouts, conditions and other relevant
information relative to property, plant and equipment (“Remarks” column). Absence
of information on those fields cast doubt on the result of actual count undertaken on
those PPEs as well as the completeness of physical count made by the City.

Instructions on the accomplishment of the RPCPPE included in MNGAS for LGUs,
Volume II specifically states the following:

“This form shall be accomplished as follows:
xxx
c. Name of Accountable Officer – name of the accountable officer
d. Official Designation – official designation of the accountable officer
f. Date of Assumption – first day of assumption of the duties and
responsibilities as accountable officer
g. Article – type of item or article e.g. personal computer, printer, filing
cabinet, etc.
i. Property Number – assigned property number by the Supply and
Property Unit
j. Unit of Measure – e.g. piece, set, etc.
l. Balance per Card – quantity of items or articles appearing in the
Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Card.
m. On Hand per Count – quantity of items or articles per physical count
n. Shortage/Overage – excess of the quantity and total value of items
or articles per Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Card over that of
the physical count or vice versa.
o. Remarks – whereabouts, conditions and other relevant information
relative to property, plant and equipment”

Further, as has been the practice of the City, the Reports on the Physical Count for
the fixed or permanent assets (e.g., Buildings, School Buildings, Hospital and Health
Centers, Other Structures, Markets, and all Infrastructure Assets, etc.) were just a
mere listing of the items capitalizable with the accounts and not in full conformity with
Annex 49 in form and in contents.

The said observation has been noted repeatedly signifying the continuous disregard
of the City on the provisions of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II to accomplish the
RPCPPE completely and in the prescribed format, to indicate the actual result of
the physical count undertaken and for the reliable comparison of the report to that of the
records of the Accounting.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor instruct the City GSO
to:

a) prepare the RPCPPE in conformity with the format and contents prescribed
by Annex 49, Chapter II of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II, and ensure that the
same be submitted to the Auditor concerned on or before January 31 of
each year; and

b) completely fill up and include the vital information on the RPCPPE
pertaining the actual result of the physical inventory count undertaken for
the reliable comparison of the report with the records of the Accounting
Office.

E. Unserviceable properties with gross amount of P51.073 million were still not
disposed within reasonable time, contrary to COA Circular No. 89-296 dated
January 27, 1989, Sections 165 and 166 of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated
October 20, 1992 and Section 79 of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445, thereby
causing their rapid deterioration and depriving the City of generating
additional income from their scrap, and may pose risk of possible asset
misappropriation.

COA Circular No. 89-296 dated January 27, 1989 provides that the full and sole
authority and responsibility for the disposal of property and other assets shall be
lodged in the head of the agency who shall constitute the appropriate committee or
body to undertake the same.

Likewise, Sections 165 and 166 of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992,
the Rules and Regulations on Supply and Property Management in the Local
Governments, provides that supplies or property which have become unserviceable
or no longer needed shall be sold, whenever applicable, at public auction, subject to
applicable rules and regulations. However, for justifiable reasons, property may also
be disposed in the following manner:

a.) Sale through negotiation
b.) Transfer to other government agencies
c.) Destruction or condemnation

Further, Section 79 of PD No. 1445 provides:

“When government property has become unserviceable for any cause or
is no longer needed, it shall, upon application of the officer accountable
therefore, be inspected by the head of the agency or his authorized
representative xxx and if found valueless or unsalable, it may be
destroyed. If found valuable, it may be sold at public auction to the
highest bidder under the supervision of the proper committee on award or
similar body xxx. In the event that public auction fails, the property may
be sold at a private sale at such price as may be fixed by the same
committee or body concerned.”

Review of the submitted reports regarding the City’s PPE accounts revealed that
unserviceable properties with net gross amount of P51,073,917.51 were still
undisposed within reasonable time and were still carried in the books of accounts as
of December 31, 2021, to wit:
Account Name Amount
Office Equipment P2,219,036.00
Furniture and Fixtures 399,733.71
IT Equipment and Software 7,848,936.05
Communication Equipment 1,260,878.50
Hospital Equipment 2,135,900.00
Other Machinery and Equipment 10,451,689.30
Motor Vehicles 26,757,743.95
Total P51,073,917.51

These unserviceable properties which are no longer needed are still carried in the
books due to the failure of the City to dispose them immediately. Continuous
inclusion of their carrying amounts on the total balance of the PPEs as of year-end
notwithstanding the fact that they already lost the qualification to be recognized as
assets had overstated the PPE accounts, which affect the fair presentation of the
financial statements, and may mislead the users of the report on the actual condition
of the City’s properties.

PPEs are assets highly susceptible to physical deterioration, misuse and
exploitation, thus certain rules are placed to protect them from such risk. Regulations
were implemented by the government such as MNGAS, COA Circulars, and
international standards adopted in the Philippine settings such as International Public
Sector Accounting Standard (IPSAS) 17 complemented with IPSAS 21 providing
universal guidelines in accounting, recording and proper valuation of PPEs.

Deviation from these standards undermines the effectiveness of the controls set by
the said standards, and therefore is detrimental to the safekeeping of the PPEs.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the Chairman of
the Committee on Disposal to initiate/facilitate the immediate disposal of the
subject properties, in accordance with the afore-cited provisions, in order not
to expose those properties to elements that cause further deterioration or loss
of salvaged value and to generate additional income thereof.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to:
